options: The AWS Config service role does not have permission to evaluate S3 bucket policies; add s3:GetBucketPolicy to the Config service role to enable evaluation

The SSM Automation remediation document's execution role is missing s3:PutPublicAccessBlock permission; add this permission to the remediation execution role so it can apply the PutPublicAccessBlock API call on non-compliant buckets
AWS Config automatic remediation requires a manual approval step; configure Config to send SNS notifications and have operators approve remediation before SSM Automation executes
The Config rule evaluation frequency is set to 24 hours; change to triggered evaluation so Config detects violations immediately and triggers remediation faster
explain: AWS Config automatic remediation executes SSM Automation documents using an IAM execution role. If the remediation document calls S3 PutPublicAccessBlock but the execution role lacks s3:PutPublicAccessBlock permission, the remediation fails with Access Denied. The fix is to add this permission to the IAM role specified as the AutomationAssumeRole in the remediation configuration. The Config service role is separate from the remediation execution role — Config needs only read permissions for evaluation, while the SSM Automation role needs write permissions for remediation. Config automatic remediation does not require manual approval. Evaluation frequency affects detection speed, not remediation success.

q: A company uses AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to manage application configuration. Developers have access to /dev/* parameters but must not access /prod/* parameters. The security team discovers developers are accessing production parameters by guessing parameter names. Which IAM configuration prevents this?
options: Apply IAM policies with Deny on ssm:GetParameter for resources matching arn:aws:ssm:*:*:parameter/prod/*; attach this policy to all developer IAM roles
Use Parameter Store parameter policies to set an expiration date on /prod/* parameters, forcing rotation that renders guessed parameter names invalid

Use Systems Manager Parameter Store SecureString type for all /prod/* parameters; SecureString parameters cannot be read by IAM principals without explicit KMS key access regardless of ssm:GetParameter permissions
Enable Parameter Store advanced tier for /prod/* parameters; advanced tier parameters have resource-based policies that restrict access independently of IAM
explain: IAM resource-based Deny statements for ssm:GetParameter with a resource ARN matching the /prod/* prefix prevent developers from accessing any production parameter regardless of whether they know the name. IAM Deny always overrides Allow — even if a developer has a broad Allow on ssm:GetParameter:*, the explicit Deny on production parameters blocks access. Parameter expiration policies control parameter lifecycle, not access. SecureString parameters require KMS:Decrypt permission but still need ssm:GetParameter allowed — denying the Get action is simpler and more direct. Parameter Store advanced tier supports resource-based policies for granular control but the IAM Deny approach is sufficient and doesn't require upgrading tiers.

q: A security team requires that all AWS API calls across 20 accounts in an AWS Organization are captured in a single S3 bucket for centralized audit analysis. New accounts added to the organization must be automatically included. What is the correct approach?
options: Create an organization-level CloudTrail trail in the management account with multi-region enabled and deliver logs to a central S3 bucket with bucket policy allowing CloudTrail from the organization
Deploy a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter in each account that streams to a cross-account Kinesis Data Stream writing to a central S3 bucket
Enable AWS Config across the organization to record all API calls and aggregate findings in the management account's Config aggregator
Create individual CloudTrail trails in each member account and use S3 replication rules to copy logs to a central audit bucket in the management account
explain: An organization-level CloudTrail trail automatically covers all existing and future member accounts, satisfying the automatic inclusion requirement. It consolidates logs in one S3 bucket with appropriate bucket policies. Per-account trails with S3 replication require manual setup for each new account. AWS Config records configuration changes, not all API calls. CloudWatch Logs subscription filters require per-account setup and are not automatically applied to new accounts.

q: Your organization wants to implement graceful degradation where services remain available even when downstream dependencies fail. A Lambda function that processes requests depends on DynamoDB and an external API. How should you monitor whether graceful degradation is working correctly?
options: Use AWS X-Ray traces to monitor whether requests complete successfully or fall back to degraded responses, and create CloudWatch metrics from X-Ray trace annotations indicating degraded mode
Enable CloudWatch Lambda Insights to monitor concurrency utilization and identify when the Lambda function is operating in a degraded state by correlating high concurrency with increased error rates

Use CloudWatch custom metrics published by the Lambda function itself: emit a DegradedModeCount metric when fallback logic is invoked, and create alarms when degraded mode exceeds a threshold indicating the dependency is impacted

Configure a CloudWatch Synthetics canary to periodically invoke the Lambda function and check whether it returns a degraded response by inspecting the response body for a graceful degradation indicator field
explain: The most reliable way to monitor graceful degradation is to have the Lambda function explicitly emit a custom CloudWatch metric when it invokes fallback logic. Using CloudWatch Embedded Metrics Format (EMF) or PutMetricData, the function publishes DegradedModeCount=1 each time it falls back to a cached response or default value. A CloudWatch Alarm on this metric detects when degradation is occurring at high frequency, indicating a sustained dependency failure requiring investigation. X-Ray annotations require correlation analysis. Lambda Insights tracks performance metrics, not application-level degradation mode. Synthetics canaries test from outside but cannot reliably detect whether a specific fallback code path was executed internally.

q: How should service-level objectives (SLOs) be defined and monitored for a multi-region deployment where each region independently serves users? The SLO target is 99.9% of requests completing in under 200ms, measured globally.
options: Create one CloudWatch Alarm per region monitoring the local p99.9 latency and aggregate alarm states using a Composite Alarm to represent the global SLO health
Use Route 53 health checks as the global SLO indicator; Route 53 health check failure rate directly represents the percentage of requests that violated the latency SLO
Define SLOs as CloudWatch Anomaly Detection bands on API Gateway latency; alerts fire when regional latency deviates from the seasonal baseline regardless of the 200ms threshold
Use AWS CloudWatch Application Signals to define SLOs with a p99.9 latency target of 200ms on the API service, combining data from all regions into a single SLO dashboard with error budget tracking
explain: AWS CloudWatch Application Signals provides purpose-built SLO management with percentile-based latency targets, error budget tracking, and multi-region data aggregation. Defining a p99.9 latency SLO at 200ms against the API service creates an automatically calculated error budget and burn rate metrics. CloudWatch Composite Alarms combine regional alarm states but do not track error budgets or provide SLO-specific dashboards. Route 53 health checks measure endpoint availability, not request-level latency percentiles. Anomaly Detection uses adaptive baselines, not fixed SLO thresholds.

q: Your application serves different geographic markets with strict data residency requirements — European user data must stay in eu-west-1 and US user data must stay in us-east-1. Users must be routed to the correct region automatically based on their location. Which Route 53 routing policy enforces this?
options: Latency-based routing to send users to the region with the lowest latency, which naturally routes European users to eu-west-1 and US users to us-east-1 in most cases
Geolocation routing with records for the European continent pointing to eu-west-1 endpoints and records for North America pointing to us-east-1 endpoints, with a default record for other locations
Weighted routing with equal weights between eu-west-1 and us-east-1 endpoints and client-side region detection to reinforce the correct regional assignment
Failover routing with eu-west-1 as primary and us-east-1 as secondary, relying on CloudFront geographic restrictions to enforce data residency
explain: Route 53 geolocation routing directs DNS queries to specific endpoints based on the geographic location of the requesting client. Configuring a record for 'continent: Europe' pointing to eu-west-1 and 'continent: North America' pointing to us-east-1 ensures that European users are always served by the European endpoint and US users by the US endpoint, satisfying data residency requirements. Latency-based routing optimizes for speed and could route European users to us-east-1 if it has lower latency. Failover routing does not enforce geographic routing. Weighted routing distributes randomly regardless of user location.

q: An organization needs to automatically remediate AWS Config rule violations. When an S3 bucket becomes publicly accessible, the team wants to automatically remove public access within 2 minutes without manual intervention. Which approach achieves this?
options: Create a CloudWatch Events rule matching S3 PutBucketPolicy events and invoke a Lambda that checks and removes public access policies on any policy change
Use S3 Block Public Access settings at the account level to prevent public access on all current and future S3 buckets, eliminating the need for automated remediation

Configure an AWS Config rule detecting public S3 bucket access, attach an SSM Automation remediation document that calls PutPublicAccessBlock API, and enable automatic remediation so Config triggers the document when non-compliance is detected
Enable AWS Security Hub with the CIS AWS Foundations standard and configure Security Hub automated remediation actions for S3 public access findings
explain: AWS Config with automatic remediation is the recommended approach for continuous compliance. Config detects the non-compliant bucket state and immediately triggers the attached SSM Automation document, which calls the S3 PutPublicAccessBlock API to remediate. This runs within minutes of the violation. CloudWatch Events on PutBucketPolicy catches policy changes but misses other public access mechanisms like ACLs. Security Hub automated remediation adds an additional layer with more latency. Account-level S3 Block Public Access is the best preventive control but does not remediate existing violations — it belongs as a complementary control.

q: An application uses an Auto Scaling group with a target tracking scaling policy set to maintain 70% CPU utilization. During a flash sale, the application receives 10x normal traffic in 30 seconds, causing CPU to spike before new instances are available. How should the team address this gap?
options: Configure the ASG with a minimum capacity equal to 10x the normal instance count to ensure always-on capacity for peak events
Implement scheduled scaling to pre-warm the ASG before the flash sale starts, combined with a predictive scaling policy that uses ML to forecast capacity needs, reducing cold-start latency
Increase the target tracking policy's cooldown period to allow more instances to launch before the policy re-evaluates CPU utilization
Switch to a step scaling policy with larger step adjustments so the ASG can add more instances faster when CPU exceeds 90%
explain: Flash sale traffic spikes are predictable events where reactive scaling always lags behind demand. Scheduled scaling pre-warms the ASG to handle the expected spike capacity before it begins. Predictive scaling uses historical ML patterns to proactively adjust capacity. Together they eliminate the 3-5 minute reactive scaling lag. Increasing cooldown periods makes scaling slower, not faster. Step scaling improves response but still has the reactive lag problem. Maintaining 10x normal minimum capacity is extremely expensive and wasteful during non-peak periods.

q: A company runs a multi-region Application Load Balancer setup where eu-west-1 handles 60% of traffic and us-east-1 handles 40%. CloudWatch metrics show different request rates per region. How should a unified CloudWatch dashboard display normalized request rates accounting for regional capacity differences?
options: Create separate CloudWatch dashboard widgets per region showing raw RequestCount metrics and let operators manually compare normalized values accounting for the capacity ratio

Use CloudWatch Metric Math to create expressions that divide each region's RequestCount by its capacity weight (0.6 for eu-west-1, 0.4 for us-east-1), displaying normalized per-capacity metrics side-by-side on a unified dashboard
Configure CloudWatch cross-region dashboards to display each region's metrics in separate dashboard sections without normalization
Use Amazon Managed Grafana with separate CloudWatch data sources for each region and create calculated fields in Grafana to normalize the metrics
explain: CloudWatch Metric Math enables mathematical operations on metric data within dashboard widgets. Dividing each region's RequestCount by its relative capacity weight produces normalized metrics that can be compared meaningfully regardless of raw traffic volume differences. This creates a fair per-unit-capacity comparison on a single unified dashboard without requiring additional infrastructure. Raw metric display (option A) requires manual cognitive normalization. Cross-region dashboards without normalization show the same apples-to-oranges comparison. Grafana requires licensing and additional setup when CloudWatch Metric Math solves the problem natively.

q: A development team uses feature branches in CodeCommit. They want every push to a feature branch to automatically trigger a CodeBuild unit test run, but only merges to main should trigger the full CodePipeline deployment pipeline. Which AWS service configuration implements branch-based CI triggers?
options: Configure a CodePipeline webhook that triggers on all branch pushes and uses a Lambda function in the first stage to determine whether to continue
Use CodeCommit approval rule templates to block merges until CodeBuild succeeds, then manually trigger CodePipeline after each approved merge

Create an EventBridge rule that matches CodeCommit repository events with referenceType=branch and branchName matching a pattern; route feature branch events to a CodeBuild project and main branch events to CodePipeline
Configure CodePipeline to poll all branches every minute and use a pipeline condition to skip deploy stages for non-main branches
explain: Amazon EventBridge natively captures CodeCommit repository events including push events with branch name in the event payload. A rule matching referenceType=branch and filtering on branchName can route feature branch pushes to a standalone CodeBuild project (CI only) and main branch merges to CodePipeline (full CD). This provides clean separation without overloading the deployment pipeline. CodePipeline polling all branches would create a pipeline execution per branch push, and deploy stage conditions are not a native feature. Approval rule templates gate merges but don't trigger CI. A Lambda gate in CodePipeline still executes the pipeline for every branch.

q: A DevOps team maintains 200 CloudFormation stacks across 10 AWS accounts. They want to enforce a standard tag (CostCenter, Environment, Owner) on all resources in all stacks, failing any stack deployment that omits required tags. Which mechanism enforces tag compliance at deployment time?
options: Configure AWS CloudFormation hooks with a pre-create and pre-update hook type backed by a Lambda function that validates resource tags in the CloudFormation template before the resource is created or updated; register the hook as mandatory in each account
Use an SCP that denies CreateStack and UpdateStack API calls unless the request includes the required tags; developers must tag the stack itself to pass the SCP condition
Use AWS Organizations Tag Policies to define required tags; CloudFormation automatically reads Tag Policies and fails stack deployments that violate tag requirements before any resources are created
Use AWS Config with the required-tags managed rule; Config evaluates deployed resources and marks non-compliant ones, alerting the team via SNS when required tags are missing
explain: AWS CloudFormation Hooks (CloudFormation extension type) execute Lambda functions at specific lifecycle events (before resource creation, before update, before deletion). A pre-create/pre-update hook can inspect the resource properties in the change set, validate that required tags are present, and return FAILED if they're missing — stopping the deployment before any resource is created. Hooks are registered as mandatory in the CloudFormation registry, ensuring every deployment in the account is validated. AWS Config evaluates deployed resources reactively, after they exist — it cannot prevent deployment. Organizations Tag Policies enforce tag key/value format standards on existing resources but don't fail CloudFormation deployments. SCP conditions on CreateStack check stack-level tags, not individual resource tags within the template.

q: A team wants to create a CloudWatch dashboard showing real-time error rates calculated as errors divided by total requests for their application. The metrics ErrorCount and RequestCount are published as separate metrics. Which feature enables this calculation?
options: Create a CloudWatch composite alarm that combines ErrorCount and RequestCount alarms to derive an effective error rate signal
Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query log groups and calculate error rates, then embed the query results in a dashboard widget
Create a CloudWatch custom metric that publishes the pre-calculated error rate from the application before sending to CloudWatch
Use CloudWatch Metric Math to create an expression ERRORS/REQUESTS*100 referencing both metrics in the same dashboard widget
explain: CloudWatch Metric Math allows referencing multiple metrics by ID and applying mathematical expressions like division and multiplication directly in dashboards and alarms, without application changes. Pre-calculating in the application adds complexity and a separate code path. Logs Insights queries work for log-based metrics but not for existing CloudWatch metrics, and have query latency. Composite alarms combine alarm states but cannot perform mathematical calculations on raw metric values.

q: A company wants to implement event-driven automation where all EC2 instance state changes (running, stopped, terminated) trigger notifications to a Slack channel and log entries to DynamoDB. Which architecture is most maintainable and scalable?
options: Configure EC2 Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks to publish notifications to SNS for scale-in and scale-out events only, then forward to Slack via Lambda
Deploy a CloudWatch Agent on all EC2 instances configured to monitor instance state and publish custom metrics to CloudWatch, triggering SNS when state changes are detected

Create an EventBridge rule matching EC2 instance state change events that sends to an SNS topic; configure AWS Chatbot as an SNS subscriber to forward to Slack, and a Lambda subscriber to write structured event data to DynamoDB
Write a Lambda function that polls the EC2 DescribeInstances API every minute and compares state changes against the previous DynamoDB state record, sending Slack messages for changes
explain: EventBridge natively captures all EC2 instance state change events without polling and delivers them in near real-time. Fan-out through SNS allows multiple subscribers with independent processing. AWS Chatbot integrates SNS with Slack natively with no code required. Lambda writes to DynamoDB with structured data. This serverless architecture scales to any number of instances without code changes. CloudWatch agent cannot monitor EC2 instance state changes. Auto Scaling lifecycle hooks only cover ASG-managed instance transitions. Polling with Lambda adds latency and misses rapid consecutive state changes between poll intervals.

q: A team deploys an Application Load Balancer across three Availability Zones with EC2 instances in each AZ. One AZ experiences a partial failure where instances are slow but not returning errors. Health checks are passing. Users in that AZ experience poor performance. Which ALB feature detects and mitigates this scenario?
options: Enable ALB Anomaly Mitigation (anomaly detection in target groups), which detects targets that are responding slowly compared to peers and automatically reduces traffic to slow targets without requiring health check failures
Reduce the health check interval to 5 seconds and lower the healthy threshold to 2 to more rapidly detect degraded targets and remove them
Configure ALB target group deregistration delay to 0 seconds so unhealthy targets are immediately removed from rotation when performance degrades
Enable cross-zone load balancing on the ALB so traffic from the degraded AZ is redistributed evenly across instances in all three AZs
explain: ALB Anomaly Mitigation uses request-level latency monitoring to identify targets that are responding significantly slower than their peers, even when health checks pass. It gradually reduces traffic to anomalous targets and can remove them from rotation without a health check failure. Deregistration delay affects the time to drain connections after a target is removed, not detection. Reducing health check interval helps with complete failures but not slow responses that still return 200 OK. Cross-zone load balancing helps distribute traffic evenly but does not detect slow targets.

q: A team deploys Lambda functions using AWS SAM. They need to implement canary deployments where 10% of traffic is routed to the new Lambda version for 30 minutes before full cutover, with automatic rollback if the error rate on the new version exceeds 5% during that window. Which SAM configuration achieves this?
options: Use Lambda aliases with routing configuration set to 0.1 for the new version; implement a Step Functions state machine that polls the error rate metric and updates the alias weight to 1.0 after 30 minutes if no errors occur

Configure an Application Load Balancer with weighted target groups — 10% to new Lambda version and 90% to old — then use a CloudWatch metric math expression to monitor error rates and trigger a Lambda rollback function

Set DeploymentPreference type to Canary10Percent30Minutes in the SAM template; configure a CloudWatch Alarm on the new version's error rate as the Alarms property; SAM creates a CodeDeploy deployment group that monitors the alarm and rolls back automatically if it fires

Deploy the new version to a separate Lambda function, use Route 53 weighted routing to split traffic, and configure a CloudWatch alarm that triggers an SNS notification requiring manual rollback approval
explain: AWS SAM's DeploymentPreference block integrates with AWS CodeDeploy to provide native traffic-shifting for Lambda. The Canary10Percent30Minutes preference routes 10% of traffic to the new version for 30 minutes then shifts 100% if no alarms fire. The Alarms property links CloudWatch alarms — if the error rate alarm fires during the canary window, CodeDeploy automatically rolls back to the previous version. This requires zero custom code. ALB weighted target groups do not support Lambda traffic shifting. Lambda aliases with Step Functions monitoring works but requires custom infrastructure. Route 53 weighted routing operates at DNS level, not Lambda alias level, and adds unnecessary complexity.

q: A company uses Terraform to manage AWS infrastructure and stores state in S3. Multiple developers work on the same Terraform configurations simultaneously, causing state file corruption from concurrent apply operations. Which configuration prevents concurrent state modifications?
options: Use Terraform Cloud's remote state backend, which handles locking automatically — self-managed S3 backends fundamentally cannot prevent concurrent applies
Use S3 Object Lock in governance mode on the state file; Object Lock prevents any write to the file while a lock exists, blocking concurrent applies

Configure Terraform backend with S3 for state storage and a DynamoDB table for state locking; Terraform acquires a DynamoDB lock before applying changes and releases it when complete, preventing concurrent apply operations
Configure S3 versioning on the state bucket; S3 versioning allows multiple versions of the state file to coexist and Terraform automatically merges concurrent state updates
explain: The S3+DynamoDB Terraform backend is the standard AWS-native solution for state locking. The DynamoDB table stores a lock record (containing the Terraform operation ID and timestamp) that Terraform creates before executing apply and deletes on completion. If a second apply attempts to start while the lock exists, Terraform detects the lock and waits or fails depending on configuration. S3 versioning preserves state history but doesn't prevent concurrent writes — two simultaneous applies would both write, corrupting the state. Terraform Cloud's remote backend is valid but the question asks about self-managed configuration. S3 Object Lock governs object deletion/overwriting but doesn't integrate with Terraform's locking workflow.

q: A company needs to monitor their containerized application on Amazon EKS for memory utilization at the pod level and visualize it in CloudWatch. The cluster uses EC2 node groups. Which configuration achieves this with the least operational overhead?
options: Deploy a custom metrics collector Lambda that uses the Kubernetes API to poll pod resource usage and publish metrics to CloudWatch every minute
Install Prometheus on the EKS cluster and configure a CloudWatch metric stream to forward Prometheus metrics to CloudWatch namespaces
Configure the EKS nodes to send system-level CloudWatch agent metrics and use EC2 instance memory metrics as a proxy for pod-level utilization
Enable CloudWatch Container Insights on the EKS cluster by deploying the CloudWatch agent as a DaemonSet using the AWS-provided add-on, which automatically collects pod-level metrics
explain: CloudWatch Container Insights with the managed EKS add-on deploys the CloudWatch agent as a DaemonSet automatically, collecting pod-level CPU, memory, network, and storage metrics with no custom code. The Prometheus approach requires maintaining a Prometheus server and configuring metric streams, adding operational complexity. A Lambda polling the Kubernetes API requires custom development and Kubernetes RBAC configuration. Node-level memory metrics cannot be attributed to individual pods.

q: A security team needs to respond to an AWS account compromise where an IAM access key is suspected to have been used by an unauthorized party. What is the correct sequence of remediation steps?
options: Enable GuardDuty IAM finding type to detect future credential misuse, and leave the current key active to continue monitoring unauthorized usage patterns
Change the IAM user's password to invalidate active sessions, then rotate the access key, and rely on CloudTrail logs retained in S3 for later forensic review
Delete the IAM user associated with the compromised key, recreate the user with the same permissions, and issue new access keys to restore normal operations

Immediately deactivate the compromised IAM access key, review CloudTrail logs to identify all API calls made with the key, revoke any IAM sessions created, assess unauthorized resource changes, and then rotate all credentials including regenerating a new access key for the legitimate user
explain: Key deactivation (not deletion) stops ongoing unauthorized use while preserving the key ID for CloudTrail correlation during forensic review. CloudTrail analysis reveals the scope — what resources were accessed, created, or modified. Revoking active IAM sessions (using IAM role session revocation or DenyAll policy) prevents stolen session tokens from being used. Only after forensic review should you assess and remediate unauthorized changes. Deleting the user removes CloudTrail attribution. Password change alone does not invalidate active access key sessions. Leaving the key active allows continued unauthorized access while monitoring.

q: A company needs their DynamoDB tables to be available and writable in two AWS regions simultaneously for a globally distributed application. Updates in either region must replicate to the other within seconds. Which DynamoDB feature provides this?
options: Create identical DynamoDB tables in each region and use an application-layer dual-write pattern from the backend services to keep both tables synchronized
Configure DynamoDB cross-region backup using AWS Backup with a 15-minute backup frequency and restore-on-failure automation using Lambda
Enable DynamoDB Streams and configure a Lambda trigger to replicate changes from the primary region table to a secondary region table using PutItem API calls

Enable DynamoDB Global Tables with multi-region replication, which provides active-active replication across selected regions with last-writer-wins conflict resolution and typically sub-second replication
explain: DynamoDB Global Tables provides native active-active multi-region replication with typically sub-second latency. It handles conflict resolution automatically using last-writer-wins semantics based on timestamps. Cross-region backup with restore automation is a backup/restore DR pattern, not active-active replication, and does not meet seconds-level replication requirements. Application-layer dual-write adds complexity, latency, and risk of divergence if one write fails. DynamoDB Streams with Lambda is a custom replication pattern with higher latency and operational complexity than Global Tables.

q: A DevOps engineer needs to run integration tests against a real AWS environment as part of a CodePipeline stage. The tests create temporary DynamoDB tables, S3 buckets, and SQS queues, then clean them up after. The pipeline must ensure cleanup runs even if tests fail. Which CodeBuild buildspec pattern guarantees cleanup?
options: Place cleanup in a finally: block in the buildspec YAML using a custom shell function; set the build to continue on non-zero exit codes using set +e
Place test execution in the build phase and cleanup in the post_build phase; post_build runs regardless of whether the build phase succeeded or failed
Place tests in the build phase and cleanup in a separate CodePipeline Lambda action that runs after the test action with an always-run condition
Place both test execution and cleanup in the build phase using try/finally blocks in a Python test runner script so cleanup always executes
explain: CodeBuild's post_build phase executes regardless of whether the build phase succeeded or failed — this is the intended use case for cleanup operations. If tests in the build phase fail (non-zero exit), the build is marked failed but post_build still runs, allowing DynamoDB tables, S3 buckets, and SQS queues to be deleted. Using try/finally in a Python script works within the build phase but requires the test runner to handle cleanup, coupling test code to infrastructure concerns. set +e suppresses exit code failures, preventing CodePipeline from detecting test failures. A separate Lambda action in CodePipeline doesn't have a native always-run condition — it only runs if the previous action succeeded.

q: A team uses AWS CDK to deploy infrastructure. They want to prevent developers from accidentally deploying stacks that would delete production databases. Which CDK feature prevents accidental deletion of stateful resources?
options: Set the removalPolicy property to cdk.RemovalPolicy.RETAIN on production RDS, DynamoDB, and S3 resources; RETAIN instructs CloudFormation to orphan the resource rather than delete it when the stack is deleted or the resource is removed from the stack
Configure CloudFormation drift detection on production stacks; drift detection identifies manually deleted resources and alerts the team before the deletion propagates to the stack
Use CDK Aspects to enforce that all database constructs in production stacks have deletionPolicy set to Snapshot, ensuring a final snapshot is created before deletion but not preventing deletion
Enable stack termination protection on all production stacks using cdk.Stack.terminationProtection = true; this prevents the entire stack from being deleted via CloudFormation
explain: CDK's RemovalPolicy.RETAIN instructs CloudFormation to leave the physical resource (RDS instance, DynamoDB table, S3 bucket) intact when CloudFormation would otherwise delete it — whether because the stack is deleted, the resource is removed from the template, or the resource is replaced by an update. The resource becomes an orphan (no longer managed by CloudFormation) but continues running and preserving data. This is the most direct protection against accidental data loss. Stack termination protection prevents deleting the entire stack but doesn't protect individual resources when they're removed from the template. RETAIN is stronger than Snapshot because Snapshot still deletes the live resource. CloudFormation drift detection is for detecting unauthorized changes, not preventing deletions.

q: An application writes structured JSON logs to CloudWatch Logs. The team needs to create a CloudWatch metric that counts the number of log events where the field statusCode equals 500. Which approach is correct?
options: Create a CloudWatch Logs metric filter on the log group with filter pattern { $.statusCode = 500 } and publish to a custom CloudWatch metric namespace
Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to run a scheduled query counting statusCode 500 events and store results in a DynamoDB table for alarming
Enable CloudWatch Anomaly Detection on the log group and configure it to flag anomalies in statusCode field values
Configure a Kinesis Data Firehose subscription on the log group to stream logs to S3 and use Athena to count 500 errors for metrics
explain: CloudWatch Logs metric filters use JSON filter patterns like { $.statusCode = 500 } to match structured log fields and increment a custom CloudWatch metric in real time, enabling alarms and dashboards. Logs Insights queries are interactive or scheduled but do not natively publish to CloudWatch metrics. Kinesis to S3 to Athena adds significant latency and operational complexity. Anomaly Detection works on existing numeric metrics, not on log field values directly.

q: A team needs to implement a multi-step incident response workflow that: (1) isolates the affected EC2 instance, (2) captures memory and disk forensics, (3) sends notifications at each step, and (4) waits for human approval before terminating the instance. Which service orchestrates this workflow?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager Automation with a multi-step document that runs each action sequentially and pauses at an approval step using the aws:approve action type
Configure AWS CodePipeline with manual approval actions between each incident response stage, using Lambda action providers for the automated isolation and forensics steps
Write a single Lambda function that executes all steps sequentially with built-in waits using sleep() and polls an SQS queue for human approval responses

Use AWS Step Functions with a state machine defining each step as a Lambda task state, with a wait state requiring a human approval callback (task token pattern) before the termination step, and SNS integrations for notifications at each transition
explain: Step Functions is designed for long-running, multi-step workflows with human intervention. The task token callback pattern pauses the workflow at the approval step and resumes only when a human calls SendTaskSuccess or SendTaskFailure with the token. SNS publish can be an integrated service call in state machine transitions. Each step is visible in the execution history for audit. A monolithic Lambda with sleep is limited by the 15-minute maximum execution time and lacks visual execution tracking. SSM Automation aws:approve action works but is less flexible than Step Functions for complex branching logic. CodePipeline is for CI/CD pipelines, not incident response orchestration.

q: A company uses Route 53 for DNS with a failover routing policy. The primary endpoint in us-east-1 has a health check configured. During a region failure, the failover to us-west-2 is taking 5+ minutes. What is the most likely cause and fix?
options: The ALB in us-east-1 is not forwarding health check requests to the application, causing false-positive healthy responses; configure the health check path to a deep application health endpoint
Route 53 failover routing requires manual intervention to update the primary record weight to 0 before traffic shifts to the secondary; configure an automated Lambda to update the weight

The Route 53 health check's RequestInterval is set to 30 seconds and FailureThreshold is set to 3, resulting in up to 90 seconds for detection plus DNS TTL propagation; reduce health check interval to 10 seconds and lower the threshold to speed up detection
DNS TTL is set to 300 seconds on the Route 53 records; reducing TTL to 60 seconds will eliminate the propagation delay and speed up failover
explain: Route 53 health check failure detection time = RequestInterval × FailureThreshold. With 30-second intervals and threshold 3, detection takes up to 90 seconds, plus DNS TTL propagation time. Reducing interval to 10 seconds and threshold to 2 reduces detection to 20 seconds. DNS TTL also contributes but Route 53 overrides cached DNS for health check failures, so the health check detection time is the primary bottleneck. Fixing the health check path endpoint matters for correctness but does not speed up a working health check. Route 53 failover is fully automatic and does not require weight updates.

q: A team maintains 20 microservices each with its own CodePipeline. All pipelines share the same CodeBuild project configuration but with different environment variables per service. Managing 20 separate pipeline definitions is becoming a maintenance burden. Which approach reduces pipeline definition duplication while preserving per-service configuration?
options: Create one CodePipeline and use a Lambda function in the Source stage that routes the execution to the correct build configuration based on which repository triggered the pipeline
Use AWS CodeStar Connections to synchronize all 20 pipeline definitions from a shared Git repository, enabling centralized pipeline management

Use AWS CDK to define a reusable Pipeline construct that accepts service name, repository, and environment-specific parameters; instantiate it 20 times in the CDK app — changes to the construct definition propagate to all pipelines on the next cdk deploy
Merge all 20 microservices into a single monorepo and use one CodePipeline with a CodeBuild build matrix that parallelizes builds per service subdirectory
explain: AWS CDK constructs enable DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) pipeline definitions. A reusable L3 construct encapsulates all pipeline stages, actions, and IAM roles; each microservice instantiation passes only the varying parameters (repo name, branch, environment variables). When the construct is updated (e.g., adding a security scan stage), a single cdk deploy propagates the change to all 20 pipelines simultaneously. A monorepo changes the architecture, not just the pipeline definition. A single CodePipeline with Lambda routing conflates all service deployments into one pipeline, losing independent deployment capability. CodeStar Connections manages source provider authentication, not pipeline definition reuse.

q: A DevOps team uses AWS Systems Manager State Manager to apply a configuration baseline to all EC2 instances. A new compliance requirement mandates that all instances must have the CWAgent (CloudWatch agent) installed and running. How should they implement this using State Manager?
options: Create a State Manager association using the AWS-InstallAndManageCloudWatchAgent document targeting all instances with tag Environment:Production; set the schedule to run every 30 minutes; State Manager re-applies the configuration if the agent is stopped or uninstalled
Use AWS Config with the cloudwatch-agent-enabled managed rule to detect non-compliant instances; create an SSM automation remediation that installs the agent on non-compliant instances
Use EC2 Image Builder to create a hardened AMI with the CloudWatch agent pre-installed; replace all existing instances with the new AMI to ensure compliance

Create an EC2 Auto Scaling lifecycle hook that installs the CloudWatch agent when instances launch; State Manager is only for applying one-time configuration changes, not for continuous compliance enforcement
explain: AWS Systems Manager State Manager creates persistent associations between documents and target resources. The association runs on a configurable schedule (e.g., every 30 minutes) and re-applies the document if the instance state drifts from the desired configuration. AWS-InstallAndManageCloudWatchAgent installs the agent, configures it from Parameter Store, and ensures it's running. If the agent is manually stopped or uninstalled, the next association run detects the drift and reinstalls/restarts it — providing continuous compliance rather than one-time installation. State Manager is explicitly designed for ongoing configuration enforcement. Config with remediation is reactive (detects then remediates); State Manager is proactive (prevents drift). Image Builder ensures new instances are compliant but doesn't address existing instances or agent restarts.

q: A DevOps engineer needs to detect when application latency anomalies occur without setting fixed thresholds, because traffic patterns vary significantly between weekdays and weekends. Which CloudWatch capability should be used?
options: Configure CloudWatch metric math to calculate a rolling Z-score of latency and alarm when the Z-score exceeds 3
Enable CloudWatch Anomaly Detection on the latency metric, which uses ML to build a model of expected values including seasonal patterns, and alarm when values fall outside the band
Create a CloudWatch alarm with a dynamic threshold that queries the last 7 days of data and sets the alarm threshold at 2 standard deviations above the weekly average
Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query p99 latency logs hourly and trigger an SNS alert when values exceed the rolling 24-hour 95th percentile
explain: CloudWatch Anomaly Detection trains an ML model on the metric's historical data, automatically capturing diurnal and weekly seasonality patterns. It creates a dynamic expected-value band and can trigger alarms when actual values fall outside this band, eliminating the need for static thresholds. CloudWatch does not support dynamic threshold alarms that query historical data natively. Log Insights requires custom processing and cannot directly alarm. Z-score metric math does not account for seasonal patterns automatically.

q: A team receives an alert that a Lambda function's error rate has spiked to 50%. They need to immediately roll back the function to the last known good version without service interruption. Which Lambda feature enables instant rollback?
options: Use Lambda function code rollback in the console by selecting the previous deployment package from the S3 bucket and uploading it as the current function code
Redeploy the Lambda function from the previous version's source code stored in CodeCommit, triggering a full CI/CD pipeline build and deployment
Scale up Lambda provisioned concurrency to the previous version's configuration to revert performance characteristics while investigating the issue

Use Lambda aliases pointing to a specific function version; update the alias to point to the previous stable version number, which takes effect immediately for all new invocations without deployment pipeline delays
explain: Lambda aliases are pointers to specific function versions. When an alias points to version N and version N+1 has issues, updating the alias to point to version N is an API call (UpdateAlias) that takes effect within seconds for all subsequent invocations. In-flight invocations on the new version complete but all new requests immediately hit the stable version. Re-deploying from source code takes several minutes through the CI/CD pipeline. Manual S3 upload is not a clean rollback mechanism and loses version tracking. Provisioned concurrency settings are about initialization performance, not code version control.

q: An ECS service running on Fargate needs to automatically scale based on the number of messages in an SQS queue. When the queue is empty, the service should scale to 0 tasks, and scale up based on queue depth. Which scaling configuration achieves this?
options: Use a target tracking scaling policy on the ECS service with the built-in SQS queue depth metric as the target metric and minimum capacity of 1
Create a Step Scaling policy on the ECS service with steps corresponding to SQS queue depth ranges and set the service minimum to 0 tasks

Configure ECS Service Auto Scaling with a custom metric scaling policy using a CloudWatch metric (ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible / desired task count) as the target, and set minimum capacity to 0 with Application Auto Scaling
Deploy a Lambda function triggered by SQS that manually calls UpdateService API to adjust the ECS service desired count based on queue depth
explain: ECS Application Auto Scaling supports scaling to 0 when minimum capacity is set to 0. The custom metric (queue depth divided by number of running tasks) provides a per-task work metric that scales tasks proportionally. When the queue is empty, the metric is 0 and the service scales to 0. Target tracking on ECS with SQS built-in metric does not support scale-to-zero natively without custom metric configuration. Step scaling requires defining each step manually and does not easily handle scale-to-zero. Lambda calling UpdateService is a manual approach with added latency and operational overhead.

q: A company's CodePipeline deployment to production failed because a developer accidentally committed a breaking database migration. The migration ran successfully but the new application version failed to start. The team needs to roll back the application to the previous version without reversing the database migration. Which action restores service fastest?
options: Delete the failed CloudFormation stack and redeploy the entire environment from the last successful pipeline artifact

Use CodeDeploy's rollback feature to redeploy the previous application revision to the affected instances or ECS tasks — CodeDeploy maintains the previous revision and can redeploy it without a new pipeline run
Trigger a new CodePipeline execution with the previous commit SHA by updating the pipeline source configuration to point to the last known-good commit tag
Manually SSH into production servers, revert the application binary to the previous version, and restart the service to bypass the pipeline entirely
explain: AWS CodeDeploy maintains a history of previous deployment revisions and supports rollback to any prior revision without triggering a new pipeline execution. In the CodeDeploy console or via CLI, selecting 'Roll back when a deployment fails' or manually initiating a rollback redeploys the previous application artifact to all targets in minutes. This restores the application without touching the database — the forward-compatible migration remains. Re-running the pipeline with a previous commit is slower (full pipeline execution time) and may not be possible if the previous commit isn't tagged. Manual SSH is error-prone and doesn't update deployment tracking. Deleting the CloudFormation stack causes extended downtime.

q: A company stores secrets in AWS Secrets Manager. Their application on ECS retrieves the database password at container startup by calling SecretsManager:GetSecretValue. Security team finds the secret is being retrieved on every API request instead of once at startup, causing excessive API calls and costs. What is the best practice fix?
options: Switch from Secrets Manager to Parameter Store Standard parameters, which do not have throughput limits and don't incur per-API-call charges
Enable Secrets Manager resource-based policies to throttle the application's GetSecretValue calls to one per minute, preventing excessive API usage

Use the AWS Secrets Manager agent or AWS Parameters and Secrets Lambda Extension to cache secrets locally in the container; configure a cache TTL (e.g., 5 minutes) so the container retrieves the secret from Secrets Manager once and serves subsequent calls from memory

Cache the secret in an ElastiCache Redis cluster; the application retrieves the secret once from Secrets Manager and stores it in Redis for subsequent requests, with a TTL matching the secret rotation period
explain: The AWS Parameters and Secrets Lambda Extension (for Lambda) and the equivalent Secrets Manager agent for ECS provide a local caching sidecar or layer that handles GetSecretValue calls. The application calls a local HTTP endpoint (localhost:2773) instead of the Secrets Manager API directly; the agent serves the cached value and only refreshes from Secrets Manager when the TTL expires. This dramatically reduces API calls and cost while maintaining security — the local cache is in-process memory, not an external service. ElastiCache Redis is an external network call that introduces a dependency and increases attack surface. Resource-based throttling breaks the application rather than fixing the access pattern. Parameter Store Standard has throughput limits and still incurs costs for high-frequency calls.

q: A team operates a multi-account AWS environment and needs to aggregate CloudWatch metrics and alarms from 10 accounts into a single operations account for unified visibility. Which service enables cross-account CloudWatch observability?
options: Configure CloudWatch metric streams in each source account to deliver metrics to a central Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus workspace
Create IAM cross-account roles in each account and write a Lambda in the operations account that pulls GetMetricData API calls from each source account

Enable CloudWatch cross-account observability by configuring monitoring account and source accounts with sharing links, allowing the monitoring account to view metrics, alarms, and dashboards from source accounts
Deploy CloudWatch agents in each account configured to ship metrics to a central Kinesis Data Stream in the operations account for aggregation
explain: CloudWatch cross-account observability is a native feature that lets you designate a monitoring account and link source accounts. Once linked, the monitoring account can view metrics, alarms, log groups, and dashboards from all source accounts in a unified console without data copying. Kinesis-based solutions require custom aggregation logic. Lambda-based polling adds latency and operational burden. Prometheus does not natively consolidate CloudWatch alarms and dashboards.

q: An organization uses AWS Security Hub to aggregate security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, and Config across 20 accounts. They want findings of CRITICAL severity to automatically create PagerDuty incidents within 5 minutes. Which integration achieves this with minimal custom code?
options: Export Security Hub findings to S3 using Security Hub integration, run a scheduled Athena query for CRITICAL findings, and invoke Lambda to create PagerDuty incidents every 5 minutes
Enable Security Hub automated response playbooks from AWS Solutions Library and configure PagerDuty as the notification destination in the playbook parameters

Configure EventBridge rules in Security Hub's aggregation account matching findings with Severity.Label = CRITICAL and invoke AWS Chatbot with PagerDuty integration, or send to an SNS topic with a Lambda subscriber that calls the PagerDuty Events API
Set up Security Hub custom actions that manually trigger PagerDuty incident creation when an analyst reviews and flags a CRITICAL finding
explain: Security Hub publishes findings as EventBridge events in near real-time. An EventBridge rule filtering on Severity.Label CRITICAL routes to a Lambda function that calls the PagerDuty Events API v2 to create incidents with finding context. This is event-driven and meets the 5-minute requirement. Alternatively, AWS Chatbot has native PagerDuty integration for some workflows. Custom actions require manual analyst triggering, not automated response. S3 export with scheduled Athena queries adds latency beyond 5 minutes. AWS Solutions Library playbooks exist but require configuration and custom parameter setup for PagerDuty.

q: A company has an RDS Aurora MySQL cluster with a writer instance and two reader instances in a single region. They want to improve read scalability and ensure the application can automatically reconnect to the writer after a failover without code changes. Which Aurora feature should they use?
options: Enable Aurora Global Database to replicate to a secondary region and use the primary region endpoint for all reads and writes
Deploy an RDS Proxy in front of the Aurora cluster to handle connection pooling and automatic writer detection after failover events
Configure the application with the individual instance endpoints for each reader and writer, implementing custom client-side load balancing and failover detection

Use Aurora cluster endpoint for writes (which automatically points to the current writer after failover) and reader endpoint for reads (which load-balances across all reader instances), configuring the application with both endpoints
explain: Aurora provides managed endpoints that abstract instance topology: the cluster endpoint always points to the current writer (automatically updated after failover within 30 seconds of DNS propagation) and the reader endpoint load-balances across reader instances. Applications using these endpoints require no code changes after failover. Individual instance endpoints require custom failover logic. Aurora Global Database is for cross-region replication, not read scaling in a single region. RDS Proxy helps with connection pooling but adds a layer of infrastructure; the Aurora endpoints alone meet the stated requirements.

q: A security team requires that all Docker images deployed to production pass a vulnerability scan with no CRITICAL findings. Images are built in CodeBuild and pushed to ECR. The pipeline must fail before the deploy stage if critical vulnerabilities exist. Which implementation enforces this gate?
options: Enable ECR enhanced scanning with Amazon Inspector; add a CodeBuild post-deploy stage that queries the Inspector findings API for the image digest and fails the build if CRITICAL findings exist
Enable AWS Security Hub and create a custom insight that counts CRITICAL ECR findings; use a Lambda function to poll Security Hub and send a CodePipeline approval rejection when the count is non-zero

Enable ECR basic scanning on push; add a CodeBuild action after the Docker push that calls ecr:DescribeImageScanFindings and exits non-zero if any finding has severity CRITICAL, blocking the downstream deploy stage
Configure Amazon Macie to scan the ECR repository for sensitive data and block deployments when Macie findings exceed a threshold severity
explain: ECR basic scanning (or enhanced scanning with Inspector) runs automatically on image push and populates findings via DescribeImageScanFindings API. A CodeBuild action immediately after the ECR push can call this API, parse the findingSeverityCounts for CRITICAL, and exit non-zero if any exist — which marks the CodeBuild action FAILED and halts CodePipeline before the deploy stage. This is synchronous, fast (scan results available within seconds of push), and requires no additional services. A post-deploy stage scan runs after deployment, defeating the pre-deploy gate purpose. Macie scans for sensitive data in S3, not container vulnerabilities in ECR. Security Hub aggregation adds latency and complexity compared to a direct ECR API call.

q: A team manages a large CloudFormation template with 400 resources. Template updates frequently fail due to the 500-resource limit per stack. The team also wants independent deployment of logical application tiers (networking, security, application). Which CloudFormation pattern addresses both concerns?
options: Split the template into nested stacks using AWS::CloudFormation::Stack resources; each nested stack manages one tier (networking, security, application) with cross-stack references via stack outputs
Split into separate stacks and use CloudFormation exports/imports between them; each stack exports its outputs and dependent stacks import using Fn::ImportValue
Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets to deploy each tier as a separate stack instance across multiple regions simultaneously
Use AWS Service Catalog to create products for each tier; Service Catalog stacks can reference each other without the 500-resource limitation
explain: Nested stacks (AWS::CloudFormation::Stack resources within a parent stack) allow decomposing a large monolithic template into logical units while maintaining parent-child lifecycle management. Each nested stack can have up to 500 resources, and the parent stack orchestrates all nested stacks as a unit — updating the parent template triggers updates to all nested stacks. Cross-stack references within nested stacks use the GetAtt function on the nested stack's Outputs. This maintains coupled lifecycle management. CloudFormation exports/imports (cross-stack references) decouple stack lifecycles, which is good for independent deployment but creates hard dependencies — deleting an exporting stack fails if another stack imports its value. StackSets deploy to multiple accounts/regions, not for splitting resources within one account. Service Catalog creates catalog items, not a resource decomposition pattern.

q: A company wants to proactively detect when their website experiences increased error rates or latency degradation from a user experience perspective, before backend alarms fire. They want to simulate user interactions from multiple AWS regions. Which service should they use?
options: Set up AWS Global Accelerator health checks on application load balancers across regions to detect endpoint failures proactively
Deploy CloudWatch RUM on the website to collect real user browser performance data and create alarms on p99 page load time exceeding thresholds
Create Route 53 health checks targeting application endpoints from multiple AWS edge locations with failover configured to a backup region
Configure CloudWatch Synthetics canaries with Node.js or Python scripts that simulate user flows, run on a schedule from multiple regions, and trigger alarms on failures or latency thresholds
explain: CloudWatch Synthetics canaries are fully managed synthetic monitors that run scripted user flows (login, checkout, navigation) on a schedule from configurable AWS regions, providing outside-in monitoring before real users are impacted. CloudWatch RUM collects real user data but is reactive to actual user impact. Route 53 health checks test endpoint availability but cannot simulate complex multi-step user flows. Global Accelerator health checks detect routing-level failures, not application-level user experience issues.

q: A team needs to analyze VPC Flow Logs to determine the full communication pattern of a potentially compromised EC2 instance for forensic purposes. The logs are in S3 partitioned by date. They need to query 30 days of data quickly. Which tool is most appropriate?
options: Use Amazon Athena to query VPC Flow Logs in S3 with partition projection configured for the date-based prefix structure, enabling SQL queries filtering by instance IP address across 30 days of data without loading data into a database
Stream all 30 days of VPC Flow Logs from S3 to Amazon OpenSearch Service and use Kibana to build visualizations of the instance's communication patterns
Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query the VPC Flow Logs stored in CloudWatch Logs with a time range filter of 30 days for the specific instance IP
Download all VPC Flow Log files from S3 for the 30-day period to a local workstation and use grep and awk to filter for the instance IP address
explain: Athena with partition projection queries S3 data directly without ETL, supporting SQL with WHERE clauses filtering by source or destination IP. Partition projection eliminates partition maintenance and enables efficient date-range queries across 30 days of data. It scales to handle terabytes without infrastructure management. Downloading to a workstation is impractical for 30 days of production VPC Flow Logs which can be terabytes in size. Streaming 30 days of historical data to OpenSearch requires significant time and cost before querying. CloudWatch Logs Insights supports 30-day queries but only if the VPC Flow Logs are in CloudWatch Logs (not S3), and the query engine may time out on large volumes.

q: A media streaming service needs to serve content globally with consistent performance for users in Asia, Europe, and North America. The origin is an S3 bucket in us-east-1. Content updates must be reflected globally within 10 minutes. Which architecture provides the best performance globally?
options: Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the us-east-1 bucket, which routes uploads and downloads through CloudFront edge locations for global acceleration
Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication to replicate content to buckets in ap-southeast-1 and eu-west-1, then use Route 53 latency-based routing to direct users to the nearest regional S3 bucket

Deploy CloudFront with S3 as origin, configure cache behaviors with a TTL of 600 seconds (10 minutes) for media files, enable CloudFront origin shield in us-east-1 to reduce origin load, and use CloudFront's global edge network for consistent worldwide performance
Deploy Application Load Balancers in each region with EC2 instances that proxy requests to the us-east-1 S3 bucket, using Route 53 latency routing to direct users to the nearest ALB
explain: CloudFront with 400+ global edge locations provides the lowest latency for media streaming worldwide. TTL of 600 seconds ensures content updates propagate within 10 minutes as cached content expires. Origin Shield adds a regional caching layer to reduce origin load during cache misses. S3 CRR with Route 53 requires users to always fetch uncached content from regional S3 endpoints and adds replication lag. ALB proxy adds latency and cost without CDN benefits. S3 Transfer Acceleration optimizes upload speed, not content delivery to end users.

q: A team uses AWS CodeArtifact to host internal Python packages. A CodeBuild project installs dependencies from CodeArtifact during the build phase. Builds intermittently fail with '401 Unauthorized' errors when fetching packages. The CodeBuild service role has the correct CodeArtifact permissions. What is the most likely cause?
options: CodeArtifact requires VPC endpoints; without a VPC endpoint the authentication token cannot be validated even with correct IAM permissions
The CodeBuild project is using a custom build environment image that does not have the AWS CLI pre-installed, preventing token generation
CodeBuild cannot access CodeArtifact in a different AWS region; the team must move the CodeArtifact domain to the same region as the CodeBuild project
CodeArtifact tokens expire after 12 hours by default; if the CodeBuild project caches pip packages and the cache is older than 12 hours, the cached auth token embedded in pip.conf is no longer valid
explain: AWS CodeArtifact authorization tokens are time-limited — by default they expire after 12 hours (configurable up to 12 hours maximum). The standard CodeBuild setup calls aws codeartifact get-authorization-token at build start to generate a fresh token and configures pip with it. If CodeBuild caches the pip configuration (including an embedded expired token) and reuses the cache across builds, subsequent builds fail with 401 because the cached token is expired. The fix is to always regenerate the token in the install or pre_build phase rather than relying on cached pip configuration. CodeArtifact supports cross-region access. The AWS CLI is pre-installed in AWS managed CodeBuild images. VPC endpoints are optional for CodeArtifact access.

q: A DevOps engineer uses AWS Config to monitor compliance across 20 AWS accounts. A new security requirement mandates that no EC2 security group should allow unrestricted SSH (port 22) access from 0.0.0.0/0. Which Config setup detects and auto-remediates violations?
options: Use Amazon GuardDuty to detect security groups with unrestricted SSH access; configure GuardDuty findings to trigger a Lambda function that removes the 0.0.0.0/0 ingress rule

Enable the restricted-ssh AWS Config managed rule in all 20 accounts using a Config aggregator; create a Systems Manager Automation remediation action using the AWS-DisablePublicAccessForSecurityGroup document; configure automatic remediation on the Config rule
Create a custom Lambda-backed Config rule that evaluates security group inbound rules; configure SNS notifications for non-compliant resources; require manual remediation by the security team
Use a Service Control Policy that denies ec2:AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress for port 22 from 0.0.0.0/0; SCPs prevent the violation from being created rather than detecting it after the fact
explain: The AWS Config managed rule restricted-ssh is pre-built to detect security groups with unrestricted inbound access on port 22. Enabling it via a Config aggregator across all 20 accounts with the AWS Organizations integration applies it to all accounts without per-account configuration. AWS Systems Manager Automation provides native Config remediation integration — selecting AWS-DisablePublicAccessForSecurityGroup as the automatic remediation action removes 0.0.0.0/0 ingress rules from non-compliant security groups without custom code. A custom Lambda Config rule reinvents the existing managed rule. GuardDuty detects threats from active exploitation, not configuration compliance. SCPs prevent future violations but don't remediate existing non-compliant security groups already in place.

q: An operations team wants to combine three individual CloudWatch alarms — CPU high, memory high, and disk high — into a single alarm that only fires when at least two of the three conditions are true simultaneously. Which feature supports this?
options: Create a CloudWatch Metric Math expression that sums the three alarm state metrics and alarms when the sum exceeds 2
Create a CloudWatch Composite Alarm using an alarm rule with the expression combining the three alarms using AND/OR logic to require at least two in ALARM state
Configure a CloudWatch dashboard widget that displays all three alarms and manually review for correlated alarm states
Use EventBridge to listen for all three alarm state change events and invoke a Lambda that counts simultaneous alarms and publishes a synthetic metric
explain: CloudWatch Composite Alarms support alarm rules with boolean logic (ALARM, OK, INSUFFICIENT_DATA states) using AND/OR operators. You can write a rule like (ALARM(cpu) AND ALARM(memory)) OR (ALARM(cpu) AND ALARM(disk)) OR (ALARM(memory) AND ALARM(disk)) to require at least two conditions. Metric Math operates on metric values, not alarm states. The EventBridge Lambda approach adds latency and requires custom development. Dashboard review is manual and not automated alarming.

q: A company wants to detect when new IAM roles with admin-level permissions are created in any account in their AWS Organization. Detection must occur within 2 minutes of the event. Which solution achieves this?
options: Enable GuardDuty's UnauthorizedAccess:IAMUser/InstanceCredentialExfiltration finding type which triggers on suspicious IAM admin role creation events

Configure an organization-level CloudTrail trail sending management events to CloudWatch Logs, create a metric filter matching CreateRole events, and use a CloudWatch Alarm on the metric to trigger SNS within 2 minutes of the API call
Schedule a daily Lambda function that calls IAM ListRoles API across all accounts and compares role policies against a known-good baseline, sending alerts for new admin roles
Enable AWS Config with the iam-no-inline-policy-blocked managed rule across all accounts in the organization using a conformance pack deployed via StackSets
explain: CloudTrail captures CreateRole events across all accounts in near real-time when an organization trail is configured. A CloudWatch Logs metric filter matching CreateRole events and IAM policy conditions triggers a CloudWatch Alarm within 1-2 minutes of the API call, which sends SNS notifications. This provides sub-2-minute detection. AWS Config rules evaluate on configuration change but with some delay; they also detect existing non-compliant roles rather than specifically new role creation events. Daily Lambda polling misses the 2-minute requirement by design. GuardDuty does not have a finding type specific to admin role creation events.

q: A company runs a stateful web application on EC2 instances behind an ALB. Users lose their sessions when instances are replaced during Auto Scaling events. What is the recommended solution?
options: Configure Auto Scaling instance refresh with a minimum healthy percentage of 100% so existing sessions complete before instances are replaced
Enable ALB sticky sessions (session affinity) so each user is consistently routed to the same EC2 instance, preserving in-memory session state
Increase the EC2 instance termination protection during scaling events so instances are not terminated until sessions naturally expire
Externalize session state to ElastiCache Redis, with the application storing and retrieving sessions by session ID so any instance can serve any request without local session state
explain: Externalizing session state to ElastiCache Redis makes the application truly stateless — any instance can serve any request by looking up the session in Redis. This enables seamless scaling without session loss. ALB sticky sessions maintain affinity to specific instances, but sessions are lost when that instance is terminated, and sticky sessions can create load imbalance. Termination protection prevents scaling-in, defeating the purpose of Auto Scaling. Instance refresh with 100% healthy percentage delays rolling updates but cannot prevent eventual instance termination.

q: A company uses GitHub as its source control system and AWS CodePipeline for deployments. After a recent GitHub API deprecation, all CodePipeline pipelines using the GitHub v1 action (OAuth) stopped triggering on push events. What is the correct migration path to restore automatic triggers?
options: Update the GitHub OAuth token stored in AWS Secrets Manager with a new personal access token; the v1 action will resume functioning with a fresh token
Migrate from the GitHub v1 action to GitHub v2 using AWS CodeStar Connections; CodeStar Connections uses a webhook-based integration that does not rely on the deprecated OAuth polling mechanism
Create a GitHub Actions workflow that calls the AWS CLI CodePipeline start-pipeline-execution command on every push event to replace the deprecated trigger mechanism
Switch from GitHub to AWS CodeCommit to eliminate dependency on GitHub API versions and ensure long-term pipeline reliability
explain: The GitHub v1 action used OAuth tokens with periodic polling, which was deprecated by GitHub. The GitHub v2 action uses AWS CodeStar Connections, which establishes a webhook-based integration directly between GitHub and AWS. Webhooks push events to CodePipeline in real time without polling, eliminating the OAuth dependency. Migrating requires creating a CodeStar Connection, installing the AWS Connector for GitHub app, and updating the pipeline source action type from GitHub (v1) to GitHub (v2). Refreshing the OAuth token does not fix the deprecated polling mechanism. A GitHub Actions workflow calling start-pipeline-execution works but loses native CodePipeline source tracking. Migrating to CodeCommit is a major architectural change that doesn't address the root problem.

q: A company runs a microservices architecture on ECS. Each service has different configuration requirements that change independently. The team uses SSM Parameter Store for configuration. When a parameter changes, the ECS tasks need to pick up the new value without restarting. Which pattern enables dynamic configuration updates without task restarts?
options: Mount the SSM Parameter Store values as environment variables in the ECS task definition; ECS automatically refreshes environment variables when the underlying parameter changes

Use AWS AppConfig with a CodeDeploy deployment strategy to manage configuration; AppConfig validates configuration changes before deployment and supports controlled rollout; integrate the AppConfig SDK in each service for polling with built-in caching and rollback

Implement a configuration polling pattern in each service: fetch the relevant parameter every 60 seconds using the SSM GetParameter API with the CacheTTL set to 60 seconds; update the in-memory configuration when the value changes without restarting the service

Configure ECS tasks to read from Parameter Store at startup; implement a parameter change detection Lambda that calls ecs:UpdateService with force-new-deployment when parameters change, triggering rolling task replacement
explain: AWS AppConfig is purpose-built for dynamic application configuration with safety features: validation (JSON schema or Lambda validator), deployment strategies (linear or canary rollout across fleet), and automatic rollback if CloudWatch alarms fire during a configuration deployment. The AppConfig SDK handles polling (configurable interval), caching (reducing API calls), and in-process configuration updates without restarts. AppConfig's deployment history provides a complete audit trail. Polling SSM Parameter Store directly every 60 seconds works but lacks validation, controlled rollout across multiple tasks, and automatic rollback. ECS environment variables are set at task startup and don't update without a new task deployment — ECS does not refresh environment variables from Parameter Store dynamically. Force-new-deployment causes task restarts, which the question explicitly wants to avoid.

q: A team needs to analyze VPC Flow Logs to identify the top 10 source IP addresses generating the most rejected traffic over the past 24 hours. The logs are stored in CloudWatch Logs. Which tool provides the fastest interactive analysis?
options: Export the CloudWatch Logs to S3 and use Amazon Athena with a CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE query and SELECT top 10 rejected sources
Use CloudWatch Logs Insights with a query filtering action=REJECT, then use stats sum(bytes) by srcAddr and sort descending with limit 10
Use CloudWatch Contributor Insights rules on the VPC Flow Log group configured to track the top sources by rejected packet count
Stream VPC Flow Logs to Amazon OpenSearch Service and use the Kibana interface to create an aggregation on source IP filtered by action
explain: CloudWatch Logs Insights provides interactive, serverless SQL-like queries directly against CloudWatch Logs groups with results in seconds. The query can filter VPC Flow Log fields, aggregate by srcAddr, and sort by traffic volume without any export or infrastructure setup. Athena requires exporting logs to S3 first. Contributor Insights is good for ongoing top-N monitoring but requires pre-configuration. OpenSearch requires streaming setup and infrastructure provisioning before analysis.

q: An incident response team needs to capture the volatile memory of a potentially compromised EC2 Linux instance for forensic analysis without shutting down the instance or modifying its running state. Which approach is correct?
options: Stop the EC2 instance to freeze its state, take an EBS snapshot, then restart the instance and analyze the snapshot for memory artifacts

Use AWS Systems Manager Run Command to execute a memory acquisition tool (such as LiME or avml) on the instance that dumps memory to a mounted EBS volume, then detach the EBS volume and attach it to a forensic analysis instance in an isolated VPC
Create an EC2 AMI from the running instance which captures a memory image alongside the disk image in a single snapshot operation
Enable EC2 instance connect and SSH into the instance to run ps aux and netstat commands, saving the output as a text file for forensic review
explain: Volatile memory acquisition requires the instance to remain running. SSM Run Command executes the memory acquisition tool remotely without requiring SSH access or modification of the instance's security groups. LiME (Linux Memory Extractor) or AVML dumps live memory to a file, which is written to a mounted EBS volume. Moving the volume to an isolated forensic instance ensures analysis does not contaminate evidence. EC2 AMIs capture disk snapshots, not RAM. Stopping the instance destroys volatile memory. Running ps and netstat provides process information but not full memory dump for malware analysis.

q: A company's Lambda function processes DynamoDB Streams events. They need to ensure the function can handle up to 1000 concurrent invocations during peak load without being throttled by other Lambda functions in the account sharing the concurrency limit. What configuration prevents throttling?
options: Enable provisioned concurrency on the Lambda function set to 1000, which pre-initializes execution environments to eliminate cold starts and guarantees capacity is always available

Configure reserved concurrency on the Lambda function set to 1000, which allocates dedicated concurrency from the account limit, preventing other functions from consuming this capacity and ensuring the function can always scale to 1000 concurrent executions
Set the DynamoDB Streams shard count to match the Lambda function's desired concurrency, as Lambda scales one concurrent invocation per shard
Configure an SQS queue between DynamoDB Streams and Lambda to buffer events, using the SQS trigger with batch size 10 to control Lambda concurrency
explain: Reserved concurrency allocates a dedicated portion of the account-level concurrency limit to a specific function. Setting 1000 reserved concurrency ensures exactly 1000 concurrent slots are available exclusively to this function and cannot be consumed by other functions.
