Provisioned concurrency pre-warms execution environments for low latency but does not prevent throttling from concurrency limits — it must be combined with reserved concurrency. Lambda with DynamoDB Streams scales based on shard count, not the other way around. SQS buffering adds latency and changes the processing architecture.

q: A DevOps team wants to implement semantic versioning for all releases. When a pull request is merged to main, the pipeline should automatically determine whether the version bump is major, minor, or patch based on conventional commit messages (feat: = minor, fix: = patch, BREAKING CHANGE: = major), tag the commit, and include the version in the build artifact. Which implementation achieves this in CodePipeline?
options: Configure an EventBridge rule to trigger a Step Functions state machine on CodeCommit push events; the state machine reads commits using CodeCommit API and creates version tags using CodeCommit PutFile action
Use AWS CodeGuru Reviewer to analyze commit messages and automatically apply version tags based on the change severity classification

Configure CodePipeline to call a Lambda function in a custom action that reads the commit history using CodeCommit GetCommit API, applies conventional commit parsing logic, determines the version bump, creates a git tag, and passes the version as an output variable to subsequent stages

Use CodeBuild with a semantic-release npm package in the build phase; semantic-release reads the commit history, determines the version, updates package.json, creates a git tag via CodeCommit API, and outputs the version for artifact naming
explain: The semantic-release tool is purpose-built for conventional commit-based version management. In a CodeBuild action, semantic-release reads the git log, parses conventional commit messages (feat:, fix:, BREAKING CHANGE:), determines the appropriate semver bump, creates a git tag via the CodeCommit API (or GitHub API), updates version files (package.json, VERSION), and outputs the new version for use in subsequent pipeline stages. It handles the complete versioning workflow without custom code. A custom Lambda function requires reimplementing semantic-release logic. Step Functions with CodeCommit API is viable but complex. CodeGuru Reviewer analyzes code quality, not commit message semantics for version management.

q: A team discovers their AWS CloudFormation stack has drifted — an engineer manually modified an EC2 security group rule outside of CloudFormation. The team wants to bring the resource back in sync with the CloudFormation template without deleting and recreating the resource. Which approach reconciles the drift?
options: Delete the drifted security group and let CloudFormation recreate it during a stack update — manual resource restoration is required when CloudFormation cannot update a drifted resource in place

Trigger a CloudFormation stack update with no template changes (using the --use-previous-template flag); CloudFormation will detect and correct the drift during the update operation by enforcing the template's desired state on all resources

Run cloudformation:DetectStackDrift to identify the drifted resource, then manually revert the EC2 security group rule to match the CloudFormation template, then run cloudformation:DetectStackDrift again to confirm the drift is resolved
Use cloudformation:ImportResource to re-import the modified security group into the stack under its original logical ID, overwriting the drift with the template definition
explain: A CloudFormation stack update — even with no template changes — causes CloudFormation to re-evaluate all resources against the template and update any resources that don't match the desired state. For a security group with an extra manually-added rule, the update removes the manual rule and enforces the template's rule configuration. Using --use-previous-template with aws cloudformation update-stack triggers this reconciliation. After the update, drift detection confirms zero drift. cloudformation:ImportResource adds resources not currently tracked by CloudFormation; it cannot re-import already-tracked resources. CloudFormation update does not automatically detect and revert drift passively — an explicit update call is required. Deleting and recreating resources causes downtime and may affect dependent resources.

q: A company needs to collect custom application metrics from EC2 instances, including memory utilization and disk I/O, and visualize them in CloudWatch. The instances run in a private subnet with no internet access. Which configuration is required?
options: Enable EC2 detailed monitoring in the console, which automatically includes memory and disk metrics at 1-minute granularity without agent installation
Deploy a metrics proxy EC2 instance in a public subnet that polls private instances via SSH and publishes metrics to CloudWatch using PutMetricData API

Install the CloudWatch agent on EC2 instances with a configuration file specifying memory and disk metrics, and configure a VPC endpoint for CloudWatch to allow private connectivity without internet access
Configure the EC2 instances to write metrics to a local file and use a Systems Manager Run Command to periodically upload metrics files to an S3 bucket for CloudWatch ingestion
explain: The CloudWatch agent collects OS-level metrics including memory and disk that standard EC2 monitoring does not provide. For private subnets, a VPC Interface Endpoint for CloudWatch (com.amazonaws.region.monitoring) allows the agent to send metrics without internet routing. Systems Manager with S3 adds unnecessary complexity and latency. A proxy instance requires maintenance and SSH access. EC2 detailed monitoring only increases polling frequency for the standard metric set and does not add memory or disk metrics.

q: A DevOps team wants to implement automated incident notification to Microsoft Teams when any CloudWatch alarm enters ALARM state across their entire AWS account. Which integration architecture requires the least custom code?
options: Configure CloudWatch Alarm to invoke a Lambda function directly that calls the Microsoft Teams webhook, bypassing SNS for lower latency
Create a Lambda function subscribed to the SNS topic that uses the Microsoft Teams Incoming Webhook URL to post formatted alarm messages to the Teams channel

Configure CloudWatch Alarm actions to send to an SNS topic, set up AWS Chatbot with a Teams channel configuration as an SNS subscriber, and enable Chatbot to forward alarm notifications formatted for Teams channels
Use Amazon EventBridge to route CloudWatch alarm state change events to an API Gateway endpoint backed by Lambda that formats and sends Teams notifications
explain: AWS Chatbot natively integrates with Microsoft Teams and Amazon SNS. CloudWatch alarms send notifications to SNS, and Chatbot as an SNS subscriber automatically formats and delivers CloudWatch alarm details (metric name, threshold, state, account) to the configured Teams channel without any custom code. Lambda with Teams webhooks works but requires writing and maintaining the formatting and HTTP post logic. Direct Lambda invocation from CloudWatch alarms works but still requires the Lambda code. EventBridge to API Gateway to Lambda adds unnecessary infrastructure layers for a task Chatbot handles natively.

q: A team needs to implement a multi-region active-active architecture for a REST API backed by DynamoDB. The application must route users to the nearest region automatically and failover within 30 seconds if a region becomes unhealthy. Which AWS service provides sub-minute failover with global routing?
options: Deploy an Application Load Balancer in us-east-1 with cross-zone load balancing enabled to route traffic to targets registered in both AWS regions

Deploy AWS Global Accelerator with endpoints in both regions and configure health checks on the ALBs; Global Accelerator detects unhealthy endpoints in under 30 seconds and reroutes traffic using Anycast IP addresses without DNS propagation delays
Configure CloudFront with two origins pointing to the regional ALBs and enable origin failover with a custom error response threshold of 30 seconds
Use Route 53 latency-based routing with health checks on both regional ALBs and set the DNS TTL to 30 seconds; Route 53 updates DNS when a health check fails
explain: AWS Global Accelerator uses Anycast IP addresses that route traffic through the AWS global network to the nearest healthy endpoint. Failover occurs at the network level within seconds, not minutes, because there is no DNS TTL to wait for. Health checks detect endpoint failures within 10-30 seconds. Route 53 failover depends on DNS TTL and client DNS caching, which can take longer than 30 seconds even with short TTLs. CloudFront origin failover is designed for content delivery, not API failover with sub-minute requirements. ALB does not support multi-region targets.

q: A team's CodeBuild project takes 18 minutes to run because npm install downloads 800 MB of dependencies on every build. The team wants to reduce build time to under 5 minutes without changing the application architecture. Which CodeBuild feature achieves the largest reduction?
options: Split the CodeBuild project into two separate projects — one that builds the Docker base image with dependencies and one that builds the application code — connected by a CodePipeline parallel action
Enable CodeBuild local caching with LOCAL_SOURCE_CACHE to cache the source checkout between builds, avoiding re-download of the repository

Enable CodeBuild S3 caching for the node_modules directory and package-lock.json; CodeBuild uploads the cache after the first build and restores it on subsequent builds when the cache key (hash of package-lock.json) matches, skipping npm install when dependencies haven't changed
Increase the CodeBuild compute type from small to large to improve download bandwidth and parallelism during npm install
explain: CodeBuild S3 caching stores the node_modules directory (and any specified paths) in S3 after the first build. On subsequent builds, CodeBuild checks the cache key (typically a hash of package-lock.json) — if the key matches, it restores node_modules from S3 and npm install completes in seconds rather than downloading 800 MB. When package-lock.json changes (new dependencies), the cache key changes and a full install runs. This is the most impactful optimization for dependency-heavy builds. Source caching only caches the source code, not dependencies. Increasing compute type improves CPU/RAM but network download is still the bottleneck. Splitting into two projects adds pipeline complexity without addressing the core dependency download issue.

q: A company has hundreds of EC2 instances that need different software installed based on their role tags (web-server, database, cache). They use AWS Systems Manager to manage configuration. Which SSM feature allows defining what configuration should be applied to instances based on their tags without scripting individual instance assignments?
options: Configure SSM Automation runbooks triggered by EventBridge rules that fire when EC2 instances are tagged; the runbook installs the appropriate software based on the tag value
Use SSM Session Manager to interactively connect to each instance group and run configuration scripts; Session Manager provides tag-based instance filtering in the console

Create SSM State Manager associations targeting instances by tag using the Key:tag:Role,Values:[web-server] target specification; each role gets its own association with the appropriate configuration document, applied continuously on schedule
Use SSM Run Command with a resource group target that filters instances by tag; Run Command applies the document to all matching instances on demand
explain: AWS Systems Manager State Manager associations support tag-based targeting — the association definition includes a target specification that selects instances where a specific tag key matches specific values. A web-server association targets Role=web-server instances, a database association targets Role=database instances, and so on. State Manager continuously applies and re-applies the association on schedule, ensuring new instances with matching tags are automatically configured when they launch without any manual intervention. Run Command is on-demand, not continuous. Session Manager is for interactive access, not automated configuration. EventBridge-triggered Automation works for new instances at launch but doesn't continuously enforce configuration on existing instances.

q: A DevOps team wants to implement real user monitoring (RUM) on their React single-page application to capture page load times, JavaScript errors, and user session data by geography. Which AWS service provides this capability?
options: Add the CloudWatch RUM JavaScript snippet to the application, configure an App Monitor in CloudWatch RUM with the application domain, and visualize performance metrics broken down by country and browser in the RUM console
Deploy a CloudFront distribution with real-time logs enabled and use Athena to query time-to-first-byte metrics by edge location as a proxy for user experience
Integrate Amazon Pinpoint into the React app to track user sessions and custom events, then query Pinpoint analytics for geographic performance breakdowns
Configure CloudWatch Synthetics with geographically distributed canaries to simulate React app page loads and measure timing from each AWS region
explain: CloudWatch RUM is specifically designed for real user monitoring of web applications. The JavaScript snippet added to the app captures browser-side timing, Core Web Vitals, JavaScript errors, and HTTP errors from actual user sessions. It provides geographic breakdown, browser, and device segmentation. Pinpoint is for user engagement and push messaging, not performance monitoring. Synthetics uses synthetic bots, not real users. CloudFront real-time logs measure CDN performance but not client-side JavaScript execution or page rendering.

q: A team discovers that an S3 bucket containing customer PII data had public read access enabled for 48 hours due to a misconfiguration. What forensic steps should they take to determine the extent of data exposure?
options: Use Amazon Macie to scan the bucket for PII data and generate a finding report showing which objects contain sensitive data for breach notification purposes
Review CloudTrail data events for the S3 bucket to see all PutObject and DeleteObject API calls during the 48-hour window to understand data modifications
Check the S3 bucket's CloudWatch metrics for GetRequests and BytesDownloaded during the period to estimate the volume of data accessed anonymously

Enable S3 server access logging (if not already enabled) and query existing access logs using Athena to identify all GetObject requests during the 48-hour window, including requester IP, object key, and timestamp, to assess data accessed
explain: S3 server access logs record every request to the bucket including the operation, requester IP, object key, response status, and bytes transferred. Querying these with Athena identifies exactly which objects were accessed, by whom (IP address), and when during the exposure window. This is essential for breach scope assessment. CloudTrail data events record API calls made with IAM credentials, not anonymous public HTTP requests. CloudWatch metrics show aggregate counts without object-level or requester detail. Macie scans for PII presence but does not show who accessed the data during the breach period.

q: A company has a three-tier web application deployed in a single AWS region. They want to improve availability from 99.9% to 99.99% (four nines). The current bottleneck is a single RDS instance. Which combination of changes achieves the availability goal?
options: Enable RDS automated backups with a 1-minute retention period and configure application-level retry logic to reconnect within 60 seconds after a failure
Move to RDS Aurora Serverless v2 which auto-scales capacity and provides higher availability through AWS-managed infrastructure
Add a read replica in the same AZ as the primary RDS instance to serve read traffic and reduce load on the primary, improving effective availability

Enable RDS Multi-AZ for synchronous standby failover (99.95% SLA), deploy the web tier in an Auto Scaling group across three AZs, and use an ALB with cross-zone load balancing, combining independent availability percentages to exceed 99.99%
explain: Four nines availability requires multi-layer redundancy. RDS Multi-AZ provides synchronous replication with automatic failover (1-2 minute RTO) and an AWS SLA of 99.95%. The web tier across three AZs in an ASG with ALB provides independent redundancy. The combined architecture exceeds 99.99% uptime. A read replica in the same AZ as the primary adds read capacity but not failover capability for the primary. Application-level retry with backups addresses recovery but doesn't improve uptime. Aurora Serverless v2 improves scaling but the availability improvement without Multi-AZ is marginal.

q: A company requires that all Lambda function code is reviewed and approved by the security team before production deployment. The code review must happen after the build stage but before deployment. Approvers must be able to view the build artifacts and leave comments. Which CodePipeline configuration supports this workflow?
options: Configure an EventBridge rule that pauses the pipeline by setting a CloudWatch Events target to call codepipeline:DisableStageTransition when a build completes, requiring the security team to manually call EnableStageTransition

Add a Manual Approval action between the Build and Deploy stages; configure the action with a notification ARN pointing to an SNS topic that emails the security team; include the CodeBuild artifact URL in the approval message so reviewers can download and inspect the built artifact
Use CodeCommit pull request approval rules to require security team sign-off on each commit before it can be merged, preventing non-reviewed code from ever entering the pipeline
Add a CodeBuild action after Build that runs automated SAST tools and blocks the pipeline if any findings exceed critical severity, replacing human review with automated analysis
explain: CodePipeline Manual Approval actions pause pipeline execution and send an SNS notification to specified reviewers. The notification message can include a URL to the CodeBuild artifact in S3 (or a custom review URL), allowing the security team to download and inspect the built artifact before approving. Reviewers can also leave comments that are recorded in the pipeline execution history. The approval times out after a configurable period (default 7 days). Automated SAST tools complement but don't replace manual security review for this requirement. Pull request approval rules gate code merges, not post-build artifact review. DisableStageTransition/EnableStageTransition via EventBridge is an anti-pattern that bypasses the native approval workflow.

q: A company stores Terraform state in S3 and wants to implement least-privilege access so that only the CI/CD pipeline role can modify state, while developer IAM roles can only read state for planning. Which S3 bucket policy configuration achieves this?
options: Use S3 Object Lock in governance mode on the state file; governance mode requires special permissions to delete objects, effectively preventing developers from modifying the state

Configure S3 ACLs on the state file granting read permission to the developer group and full-control to the CI/CD pipeline role; ACLs provide object-level access control independent of bucket policies

Create an S3 bucket policy with two statements: an Allow statement granting s3:GetObject to developer IAM role ARNs for the state file key, and an Allow statement granting s3:GetObject, s3:PutObject, and s3:DeleteObject to the CI/CD pipeline role ARN
Enable S3 Block Public Access on the state bucket and create IAM policies that grant the CI/CD role s3:PutObject and s3:DeleteObject while developers receive s3:GetObject only
explain: An S3 bucket policy combining two Allow statements — one for developer roles with s3:GetObject on the state file prefix (enabling terraform plan to read current state), and one for the CI/CD role with Get/Put/Delete (enabling terraform apply to update state) — implements least-privilege at the object level. The bucket policy supplements IAM identity policies for precise resource-level control. This allows developers to run terraform plan locally while preventing them from committing state changes outside the pipeline. Block Public Access and IAM policies work together but don't restrict within-account access as precisely as a bucket policy. Object Lock prevents deletion during the lock period but doesn't differentiate between developer and pipeline roles. S3 ACLs are legacy and AWS recommends using bucket policies over ACLs for access control.

q: An organization wants to centralize logs from 50 AWS accounts into a single Amazon OpenSearch Service domain for security analysis. Logs must be delivered with less than 5-minute latency. Which architecture is recommended?
options: Configure CloudWatch Logs subscription filters in each account to stream logs to a cross-account Kinesis Data Firehose delivery stream that delivers to OpenSearch Service in the central account
Enable CloudTrail in all accounts to write to a central S3 bucket, then configure an S3 event trigger Lambda to index logs into OpenSearch as files arrive
Use AWS DataSync to schedule hourly transfers of CloudWatch Logs exports from each account to the central account's S3 bucket, then batch-load into OpenSearch
Deploy a Logstash server in the central account and configure all EC2 instances across accounts to ship logs directly to Logstash using the Beats agent
explain: CloudWatch Logs subscription filters stream logs in near real-time to Kinesis Data Firehose, which natively integrates with Amazon OpenSearch Service. Cross-account delivery is supported through resource-based policies. This fully managed serverless approach meets the sub-5-minute latency requirement. S3-based CloudTrail delivery has higher latency due to S3 write frequency and Lambda invocation overhead. Logstash requires maintaining server infrastructure. DataSync hourly schedules cannot meet the 5-minute latency requirement.

q: A company wants to use AWS Config to continuously monitor that all EC2 security groups do not allow unrestricted SSH access (port 22 from 0.0.0.0/0). When a violation is detected, the offending rule should be automatically removed. Which Config managed rule and remediation achieves this?
options: Use the restricted-ssh AWS Config managed rule which detects security groups with port 22 open to 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0, and configure automatic remediation using the AWS-RevokeSecurityGroupIngress SSM Automation document
Enable AWS Security Hub with the AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standard which includes EC2.19 for SSH restriction and manually remediate findings
Create a custom AWS Config rule using a Lambda evaluator that calls DescribeSecurityGroups and checks for port 22 with 0.0.0.0/0 CIDR, with manual remediation notification
Configure AWS Firewall Manager with a security group policy that blocks port 22 from 0.0.0.0/0 and automatically applies compliant security group rules across the organization
explain: The restricted-ssh Config managed rule is a purpose-built AWS managed rule that evaluates security groups for unrestricted SSH access. The AWS-RevokeSecurityGroupIngress SSM Automation document is the corresponding managed remediation that automatically removes the offending ingress rule. Together they provide continuous detection and automatic remediation with no custom code. A custom Lambda rule works but requires development and maintenance when the managed rule already exists. Security Hub findings require manual remediation by default. Firewall Manager is excellent for preventive enforcement across organizations but does not directly remediate existing security group rules.

q: A company runs microservices on EKS and needs to ensure that a failure in one service does not cascade to other services. Dependent services continue to receive 503 errors when calling a failing downstream service. What pattern should be implemented?
options: Implement the circuit breaker pattern using a service mesh like AWS App Mesh or Istio, which automatically opens the circuit to a failing service and returns fast failures rather than allowing requests to queue and exhaust thread pools
Configure EKS pod disruption budgets on all services to ensure a minimum number of healthy pods are available during rolling updates and node failures
Implement exponential backoff with jitter in all service clients and set maximum retry limits of 3 attempts before returning an error to the caller
Enable EKS cluster autoscaler to add additional nodes when pod scheduling pressure increases due to failed pods consuming retry traffic
explain: The circuit breaker pattern prevents cascading failures by monitoring the error rate to downstream services and opening the circuit when the threshold is exceeded, immediately returning fallback responses without attempting the failing call. AWS App Mesh or Istio implement circuit breaking at the service mesh layer without application code changes. Pod Disruption Budgets protect availability during planned disruptions but do not prevent cascading failures from unhealthy services. Exponential backoff reduces pressure but queued retries can still exhaust resources. Autoscaling adds nodes but doesn't resolve the root cascading failure pattern.

q: A DevOps team deploys to ECS using CodeDeploy blue/green. After each deployment, the team manually runs smoke tests against the new (green) task set before confirming the deployment. They want to automate the smoke tests in CodeDeploy lifecycle hooks. Which CodeDeploy hook runs after traffic shifts to the green task set but before the blue task set is terminated?
options: AfterAllowTraffic hook, which runs after traffic has been shifted to the green task set, allowing post-shift smoke tests while the blue task set is still running
BeforeAllowTraffic hook, which runs after the replacement task set is registered with the load balancer but before traffic is shifted to it
AfterInstall hook, which runs after the application files are installed on the instances but before the application starts
BeforeInstall hook, which runs before the new task set receives any traffic and allows pre-deployment validation
explain: In CodeDeploy ECS blue/green deployments, the AfterAllowTraffic hook fires after CodeDeploy has shifted production traffic to the new (green) task set. At this point the green tasks are serving live traffic and the old (blue) tasks are still running but idle. This window is ideal for smoke tests — the tests run against the live production endpoint, and if they fail, CodeDeploy can still roll back to the blue task set before it's terminated. BeforeInstall runs before the replacement task set is created. BeforeAllowTraffic runs after the task set is created but before traffic shifts — useful for pre-shift validation but doesn't test post-shift behavior. AfterInstall applies to EC2/on-premises deployments, not ECS.

q: A team needs to manage AWS Secrets Manager secrets across 50 AWS accounts in an organization. When a new account is created, the team wants standard secrets (database passwords, API keys) automatically provisioned in the new account. Which approach automates secret provisioning for new accounts?
options: Use AWS Secrets Manager resource-based policies to share secrets from a central account to all organization member accounts; new accounts automatically receive access to centrally-managed secrets

Manually provision secrets in each new account using a runbook; automate the runbook execution by creating an EventBridge rule that triggers when new accounts are created via Control Tower or Organizations

Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets with the Organization deployment model; when a new account joins a target OU, StackSets automatically deploys a CloudFormation stack that creates the required Secrets Manager secrets in the new account
Configure Secrets Manager replication to copy secrets from a primary account to all member accounts; replication runs continuously and propagates new secrets to all accounts automatically
explain: AWS CloudFormation StackSets with the SERVICE_MANAGED deployment model integrates with AWS Organizations. Configuring auto-deployment for a target OU means CloudFormation automatically deploys a designated stack instance to any new account that joins or is created within that OU. The stack template defines the Secrets Manager secrets (with values from Parameters or SSM cross-account references) to create in each account. This requires no manual intervention for each new account. A manual runbook requires human action per account — an EventBridge rule can trigger the runbook but adding it to the automation path is an improvement, not a manual step. Secrets Manager resource-based policies share access to existing secrets; they don't create new secrets in member accounts. Secrets Manager replication copies secrets within a single account across regions, not across AWS accounts.

q: A team is using AWS Distro for OpenTelemetry (ADOT) on their EKS workloads. They want to collect traces and send them to both AWS X-Ray and a third-party observability platform simultaneously. Which ADOT component enables this?
options: Enable X-Ray active tracing and configure an X-Ray group with a filter expression to forward matching traces to an EventBridge partner event bus for the third-party platform
Use the X-Ray SDK to send traces to X-Ray and separately configure OpenTelemetry SDK to send duplicate traces to the third-party platform from the application code
Deploy two separate ADOT Collector instances, one configured to export to X-Ray and another to the third-party platform, and load-balance traces between them at the SDK level
Configure the ADOT Collector pipeline with an OTLP receiver and multiple exporters — the awsxray exporter for X-Ray and an OTLP or vendor-specific exporter for the third-party platform
explain: The ADOT Collector uses an OpenTelemetry Collector pipeline architecture with receivers, processors, and exporters. Configuring multiple exporters in a single pipeline allows the same trace data to be sent to multiple backends simultaneously without code changes. Running two separate collectors wastes resources and requires SDK-level load balancing logic. Dual SDK instrumentation adds code complexity and maintenance burden. X-Ray groups with EventBridge do not natively forward trace data to third-party platforms.

q: A team wants to receive notifications in Slack when their AWS CodePipeline deployments fail in production. They want the notification to include the pipeline name, failed action name, and a direct link to the pipeline execution. Which service provides this with minimal custom development?
options: Enable CodePipeline CloudWatch metrics for failed executions and create a CloudWatch Alarm that triggers SNS, then use a Lambda subscriber to format and send Slack notifications
Configure CodePipeline with an approval action before production deployment and use SNS email notifications to alert the team, who then manually posts to Slack

Configure CodePipeline notifications using AWS Developer Tools notifications, select the pipeline failure events, choose SNS as the notification target, and set up AWS Chatbot with the Slack channel to format and deliver the notifications with execution details
Create an EventBridge rule matching CodePipeline FAILED state changes and invoke a Lambda that formats a Slack message with pipeline details and calls the Slack API using an Incoming Webhook URL
explain: AWS Developer Tools notifications (CodeStar Notifications) natively integrates with CodePipeline and supports detailed event types including individual action failures. AWS Chatbot connects to this notification system and formats messages for Slack with pipeline name, action, and execution ID with console links — no custom code required. Lambda with Slack webhooks works but requires writing and maintaining the formatting logic. CloudWatch metrics for CodePipeline do not capture action-level failure details. Manual email-to-Slack is not automation and does not meet the notification requirement.

q: A company stores critical data in S3 and needs to ensure data is replicated to a secondary region for disaster recovery. New objects written to the primary bucket must appear in the secondary bucket within 15 minutes with 99.99% replication SLA. Which S3 feature meets this requirement?
options: Enable S3 Versioning and configure a CloudWatch Event Rule triggering a Lambda to copy new objects to the secondary bucket on PutObject events
Enable S3 Replication Time Control (S3 RTC) on the cross-region replication rule, which replicates 99.99% of objects within 15 minutes and provides replication metrics in CloudWatch
Configure standard S3 Cross-Region Replication without RTC, relying on best-effort replication typically completing within minutes for most objects
Use S3 Batch Operations to run scheduled hourly replication jobs that copy new objects from the primary bucket to the secondary region bucket
explain: S3 Replication Time Control (RTC) is specifically designed to meet compliance and business requirements for predictable replication time. It provides an SLA that 99.99% of objects are replicated within 15 minutes and publishes replication lag metrics to CloudWatch. Standard CRR has no time guarantee. S3 Batch Operations is for bulk operations on existing objects, not real-time replication of new objects. Lambda on PutObject events requires custom development and cannot guarantee the 99.99% SLA.

q: A team needs to run performance tests as part of their CI/CD pipeline after staging deployment. Performance tests take 20 minutes and require spinning up a load generation EC2 instance. After tests complete, the instance must be terminated and results stored in S3. Which pipeline architecture handles this most cost-efficiently?
options: Add a CodeBuild action that uses the AWS CLI to launch an EC2 instance with a user data script that runs the load test, uploads results to S3, and terminates itself; CodeBuild polls the instance status and proceeds when the instance terminates

Add a Lambda action that starts the performance test EC2 instance and immediately returns success; use a separate EventBridge rule to monitor instance termination and restart the pipeline from the next stage

Add an ECS Fargate task action in CodePipeline that runs the load testing tool as a container, pushing results to S3 on completion; Fargate eliminates EC2 instance management overhead while preserving on-demand cost model

Add a Step Functions action in CodePipeline that orchestrates: launch EC2 → wait for user data completion signal via SSM → collect results → terminate instance; Step Functions handles the async coordination without CodeBuild polling
explain: AWS Fargate for ECS is the cost-efficient choice for 20-minute containerized workloads: no instance management, pay only for task duration (vCPU/memory per second), and the container terminates automatically when the process exits. A CodePipeline ECS action starts the Fargate task, waits for it to complete, and treats non-zero exit codes as failures. Results are written to S3 by the container before exit. EC2 with user data polling requires CodeBuild to remain running for 20 minutes while polling (consuming CodeBuild minutes). Step Functions adds orchestration complexity for what is essentially a single container task. The Lambda split-action pattern is an anti-pattern that breaks pipeline linearity and requires custom state management.

q: A DevOps team uses AWS Service Catalog to provide pre-approved CloudFormation product portfolios to developers. A new compliance requirement mandates that all Service Catalog products must enforce specific IAM role names for execution and cannot be overridden by the end user. Which Service Catalog feature enforces this constraint?
options: AWS Organizations SCPs applied to the developer accounts preventing iam:PassRole except for the approved role ARN; this prevents developers from specifying alternative roles in any CloudFormation deployment including Service Catalog

Service Catalog launch constraints; specify a launch role ARN that Service Catalog uses to deploy the product regardless of the end user's IAM identity, preventing the user from specifying an alternative execution role
Service Catalog portfolio sharing policies; share the portfolio with a constraint policy that locks the IAM role parameter to a specific value across all member accounts
CloudFormation stack policies applied to Service Catalog products; stack policies prevent updates to stack parameters including IAM role names after initial deployment
explain: AWS Service Catalog launch constraints allow administrators to associate a specific IAM role with a product launch. When an end user launches the product, Service Catalog assumes the launch role to perform the deployment — the end user's own IAM permissions are not used, and they cannot specify an alternative execution role. This ensures all deployments use the compliant role regardless of who launches the product. Portfolio sharing policies control which accounts can access a portfolio, not which IAM roles are used for deployment. CloudFormation stack policies control which resources can be updated after deployment, not launch-time role selection. SCPs preventing iam:PassRole are overly broad and would break other legitimate CloudFormation deployments in the account.

q: A CloudWatch alarm for DynamoDB ConsumedWriteCapacityUnits is triggering frequently due to legitimate traffic spikes but the team wants to suppress alarm notifications during planned batch load windows every Sunday 2-4 AM. Which feature should be used?
options: Create a Lambda function triggered by a scheduled EventBridge rule that modifies the alarm threshold to a higher value before the batch window and resets it afterward
Add a NOT_NEEDED action type to the alarm that automatically suppresses notifications when DynamoDB table metrics indicate a batch operation is in progress
Configure a CloudWatch alarm action suppression using an alarm suppressor rule or use EventBridge Scheduler to disable and re-enable the alarm SNS action during the maintenance window
Set the alarm's evaluation period to 2 hours so that brief spikes during the batch window are averaged out and do not trigger the alarm threshold
explain: CloudWatch supports alarm action suppression through composite alarms with a suppressor child alarm, or teams can use EventBridge Scheduler to invoke SSM automation that calls DisableAlarmActions and EnableAlarmActions API calls on a schedule. Extending the evaluation period to 2 hours changes alarm sensitivity globally and may miss real incidents. Modifying thresholds via Lambda is a workable alternative but requires managing threshold state. There is no NOT_NEEDED action type in CloudWatch.

q: A security engineer needs to ensure that when GuardDuty generates a HIGH severity finding, the affected IAM user's access is automatically suspended pending investigation, without deleting the user or their permissions. Which approach achieves this?
options: Create an EventBridge rule matching HIGH severity GuardDuty findings involving IAM user entities, invoke a Lambda that attaches an explicit Deny all actions policy to the specific IAM user, suspending access while preserving all existing permission policies
Disable the IAM user account in Cognito to prevent console access, while leaving programmatic access keys active for continued investigation
Delete the IAM user's access keys immediately when a HIGH finding is generated, and recreate them after investigation is complete
Move the IAM user to a separate IAM group with no permissions, removing them from all existing groups that grant access
explain: Attaching an explicit Deny all policy (a policy with Effect: Deny on Action: '*') to the IAM user effectively overrides all other Allow policies due to IAM policy evaluation order where explicit Deny always wins. This suspends all access without modifying or deleting existing permission policies, making restoration simple — just detach the deny policy after investigation. Deleting access keys prevents programmatic access but console access with password remains. Cognito is a separate service from IAM — disabling a Cognito user does not affect IAM credentials. Moving to an empty group requires tracking and restoring original group memberships.

q: A company is designing an Auto Scaling policy for a web application. Historical data shows CPU averages 40% during normal load. The team wants the ASG to scale out when CPU exceeds 70% and scale in when it drops below 30%, with different cooldown periods to scale out fast and scale in slowly. Which policy type supports asymmetric scaling thresholds with separate cooldowns?
options: Configure a Target Tracking Scaling policy targeting 70% CPU utilization, which automatically handles both scale-out and scale-in based on the single target value

Configure a Step Scaling policy with separate scale-out steps (CPU > 70% adds instances, CPU > 90% adds more) and scale-in steps (CPU < 30% removes instances), using different cooldown periods in each scaling adjustment
Create two Simple Scaling policies — one for scale-out with a high CPU alarm and one for scale-in with a low CPU alarm — each with separate cooldown periods
Use Predictive Scaling with a CPU target of 50% and rely on ML forecasting to proactively adjust capacity before CPU threshold breaches
explain: Two Simple Scaling policies with separate alarms provide independent control over scale-out and scale-in thresholds and cooldown periods. The scale-out policy has a short cooldown (e.g., 60 seconds) for fast response, while the scale-in policy has a longer cooldown (e.g., 300 seconds) to avoid premature scale-in. Step Scaling is more powerful but supports a single direction per policy definition — separate policies are still needed for asymmetric behavior. Target Tracking uses a single target value and AWS manages both directions. Predictive Scaling is for proactive, not reactive threshold-based scaling.

q: A team manages infrastructure using AWS CDK. After a cdk deploy in the pipeline, downstream tests must use the deployed resources' ARNs and endpoints (RDS endpoint, SQS queue URL, ALB DNS name). How should the pipeline pass these dynamic values from the CDK deploy stage to the test stage?
options: Store all CDK resource identifiers in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store with fixed paths before deployment; tests read from these fixed SSM paths regardless of whether CDK updated them in the current deployment
Hard-code expected resource names in the test configuration; CDK produces predictable resource names when using explicit IDs, so tests can construct ARNs without runtime lookup
Use AWS AppConfig to store CDK output values; create a Lambda function that updates AppConfig after each CDK deploy and configure tests to fetch the latest AppConfig deployment

Export CDK stack outputs to CloudFormation; add a CodeBuild action that calls aws cloudformation describe-stacks to retrieve the output values and writes them to a JSON file stored as a CodePipeline artifact; the test stage reads the artifact file for endpoint configuration
explain: CloudFormation stack outputs are the canonical mechanism for exposing resource attributes (endpoints, ARNs, queue URLs) from CDK/CloudFormation deployments. After cdk deploy, a CodeBuild action retrieves outputs using aws cloudformation describe-stacks --query 'Stacks[0].Outputs' and writes them to a JSON file packaged as a CodePipeline output artifact. The test stage's CodeBuild action declares this artifact as an input, reads the JSON file, and sets environment variables for the test runner. Hard-coded resource names break when CDK generates names from stack/construct IDs. SSM Parameter Store requires CDK to explicitly write values to SSM, coupling the stack to SSM path conventions. AppConfig is designed for feature flags and runtime configuration, not CI/CD artifact passing.

q: A company manages 300 EC2 instances. Security team requires a daily report showing which instances are not running the approved AMI version and which instances have unapproved software packages installed. Which AWS solution produces both reports without custom scripts?
options: Use Amazon Inspector to scan all EC2 instances daily; Inspector produces findings for non-compliant AMI versions and unauthorized software packages in the Security Hub integration report

Use AWS Systems Manager Inventory to collect AMI ID and installed package data from all instances; query the inventory data using Resource Data Sync to S3 and Athena, or use the SSM Inventory console's managed instance inventory view to filter non-compliant instances

Use AWS Config with the approved-amis-by-id managed rule for AMI compliance and a custom Lambda-backed rule for software package compliance; query Config compliance status via the aggregator dashboard
Use Amazon EC2 Image Builder with a pipeline that scans existing instances and generates compliance reports comparing running instances to the latest approved AMI pipeline output
explain: AWS Systems Manager Inventory collects detailed metadata from all managed EC2 instances including the AMI ID, OS, installed applications, running services, and patch state. Resource Data Sync exports all inventory data to S3 in a structured format (JSON), enabling Athena queries to identify instances with non-approved AMI IDs or unauthorized software packages. The SSM Inventory dashboard also provides filtering views. This covers both requirements natively. Inspector scans for software vulnerabilities (CVEs), not arbitrary AMI version compliance or unauthorized package lists. Config's approved-amis-by-id rule checks AMI compliance but doesn't track installed packages; a custom package rule requires building and maintaining the Lambda function. EC2 Image Builder builds and tests AMIs but doesn't scan existing running instances.

q: An application team wants X-Ray traces to include database query details for their RDS PostgreSQL calls made through the application's ORM. X-Ray SDK is already integrated for HTTP tracing. What additional configuration is needed?
options: Enable RDS Enhanced Monitoring and configure it to send performance data to X-Ray through a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter
Enable AWS X-Ray active tracing on the RDS parameter group and configure the database to push query traces to X-Ray service
Instrument the database client by wrapping it with the X-Ray SDK's captureMySQL or capturePostgres method (or the equivalent ORM patch), enabling automatic subsegment creation for each SQL query
Create a custom X-Ray subsegment manually in application code before and after each ORM call, logging the SQL query string in the subsegment metadata
explain: The X-Ray SDK provides database client patching methods (captureMySQL, capturePostgres, or ORM-specific patches) that wrap the database driver and automatically create subsegments for each query including the SQL statement, table, and timing. RDS Enhanced Monitoring sends OS-level metrics to CloudWatch, not X-Ray traces. Manual subsegment creation works but requires code changes in every database call site. RDS does not have a native X-Ray integration — tracing must be implemented at the application layer.

q: A team uses AWS CloudTrail and wants to detect when any user assumes the OrganizationAccountAccessRole in a member account, which could indicate unauthorized cross-account access. Near-real-time detection within 3 minutes is required. Which solution achieves this?
options: Schedule a Lambda function every 3 minutes to query CloudTrail with LookupEvents API filtering for AssumeRole events and send notifications for OrganizationAccountAccessRole assumptions
Set up GuardDuty with custom threat intelligence feeds containing the OrganizationAccountAccessRole ARN to detect when this specific role is used
Enable AWS Config to evaluate IAM role assumption events and create a custom rule that checks for OrganizationAccountAccessRole assumption, with SNS notification on non-compliance

Configure CloudTrail to send logs to CloudWatch Logs, create a metric filter matching AssumeRole events where requestParameters.roleArn contains OrganizationAccountAccessRole, create a CloudWatch Alarm, and connect to an SNS topic for notification
explain: CloudTrail → CloudWatch Logs → metric filter is the standard pattern for near-real-time CloudTrail event detection. AssumeRole events are management events captured by CloudTrail, and the metric filter can match the specific role ARN in requestParameters. CloudWatch Alarms trigger SNS within 1-2 minutes of the log being delivered. AWS Config evaluates configuration state, not specific API call events like role assumptions. GuardDuty uses behavioral analysis and its own ML models — it cannot be configured with custom role ARN watchlists. Lambda polling every 3 minutes introduces up to 3 minutes of additional delay on top of CloudTrail delivery latency.

q: A company runs Aurora PostgreSQL and needs to ensure that if the writer instance fails, a reader instance is promoted to writer and the application experiences less than 30 seconds of downtime. Currently, the cluster has one writer and one reader. What configuration improves failover speed?
options: Configure RDS Blue/Green Deployments on the Aurora cluster to maintain a synchronized copy ready for instant switchover

Create the Aurora reader instance with the highest priority tier (Tier-0) in the failover priority configuration, ensure the reader has the same instance class as the writer, and enable the Aurora Fast Failover feature which uses DNS and connection heartbeat to minimize RTO
Enable Aurora Auto Scaling on reader instances to maintain at least 3 readers at all times, ensuring failover candidates are always available
Increase the number of reader instances to 5 so that Aurora has more candidates for promotion during failover events
explain: Aurora Fast Failover uses DNS endpoint updates and connection heartbeat monitoring to minimize failover time. Setting the reader to Tier-0 (highest priority) ensures it is always selected for promotion. Matching instance classes ensures the promoted reader can handle the write workload immediately. Aurora failover typically completes in 15-30 seconds with optimal configuration. More reader instances help with read scalability but do not inherently speed up failover. Auto Scaling readers helps availability but is reactive. Blue/Green Deployments are for safe database schema and version updates, not for AZ failure DR.

q: A DevOps engineer is setting up a CodePipeline for a Python application. The pipeline must enforce that code coverage never drops below 80%. If coverage drops, the pipeline must fail and prevent deployment. Which implementation achieves this with the least operational overhead?
options: Use AWS CodeGuru Profiler to measure code coverage in production and trigger a pipeline rollback via EventBridge if coverage metrics fall below threshold
Configure CodeClimate or SonarQube as an external service and create a CodePipeline webhook action that waits for the external service's quality gate result

Add a CodeBuild stage that runs pytest with the --cov flag and --cov-fail-under=80; pytest exits non-zero when coverage is below the threshold, causing CodeBuild to fail the action and halting the pipeline
Add a Lambda function that reads the coverage XML report from S3 and calls codepipeline:PutJobFailureResult if coverage is below 80%
explain: pytest's --cov-fail-under option exits with a non-zero return code when the measured coverage percentage is below the specified threshold. CodeBuild interprets the non-zero exit code as a build failure, marking the action FAILED and halting CodePipeline before any deploy stage. This requires no additional infrastructure — just the pytest-cov package and a flag in the buildspec. A Lambda function reading coverage reports adds latency and requires custom code to parse XML and interact with CodePipeline APIs. External services (CodeClimate, SonarQube) require additional setup, licensing, and network configuration. CodeGuru Profiler measures application performance in production, not unit test coverage during CI.

q: A team uses AWS CloudFormation with a CI/CD pipeline. They want to enforce that the CloudFormation template passes AWS CloudFormation Linter (cfn-lint) and CloudFormation Guard (cfn-guard) checks with zero errors before any stack deployment. The checks must run in parallel to minimize pipeline duration. Which CodeBuild buildspec pattern runs both tools in parallel?
options: Use CodeBuild batch builds with a build-list containing two separate build definitions — one for cfn-lint and one for cfn-guard — configured to run concurrently; the batch fails if either build fails
Run cfn-lint and cfn-guard as background processes using & in bash, capture their PIDs, wait for both to complete using the wait command, then check both exit codes and fail if either is non-zero
Add two separate CodeBuild actions in the same CodePipeline stage configured to run in parallel; each action runs one tool independently and the stage fails if either action fails
Run cfn-lint and cfn-guard sequentially in the build phase using && to chain commands; the second command runs only if the first succeeds
explain: CodePipeline supports parallel actions within a stage by assigning actions the same runOrder value. Two CodeBuild actions in the same pipeline stage with runOrder=1 start simultaneously — one running cfn-lint and one running cfn-guard. The stage fails if either action fails (non-zero exit). This is the cleanest parallelism without custom bash coordination. Sequential execution with && is inherently serial. CodeBuild batch builds add overhead (batch coordinator setup) and are designed for parallel matrix builds within one project, adding complexity for two simple commands. Background processes with wait work within a single CodeBuild action but require careful exit code handling and don't provide separate action-level reporting in the pipeline.

q: A team needs to ensure their CloudWatch Logs are retained for 7 years for compliance. Currently, log groups have no retention policy set. What is the most efficient way to enforce a 7-year retention on all existing and future log groups across 30 accounts?
options: Manually set retention policy on each of the 30 accounts' log groups using the AWS CLI in a bash loop script run from the management account
Write a Lambda function triggered by CloudWatch Events for CreateLogGroup events that calls PutRetentionPolicy on new log groups in a single account

Use AWS Config with a custom rule and auto-remediation using SSM Automation to detect log groups without a 2557-day retention policy and call PutRetentionPolicy to enforce it across all accounts via a conformance pack
Enable AWS Organizations Service Control Policies to deny CreateLogGroup API calls that do not include a retention period parameter in the request
explain: AWS Config conformance packs deploy Config rules and SSM Automation remediation documents across all Organization accounts simultaneously. A custom Config rule detects log groups missing the required retention (2557 days for 7 years) and the remediation automatically calls PutRetentionPolicy to fix them. This covers both existing and future log groups. A single-account Lambda only handles new log groups in one account. Manual CLI scripts are error-prone and do not scale. SCPs cannot enforce API call parameters at the CloudFormation property level.

q: A company wants to implement a post-incident review process where all major incidents automatically generate a structured post-mortem document template in Confluence. The incident data should be pre-populated from Systems Manager Incident Manager. Which integration enables this automation?
options: Configure an EventBridge rule matching Incident Manager incident resolution events, invoke a Lambda function that uses the Incident Manager API to retrieve incident timeline and impact data, then calls the Confluence REST API to create a pre-populated post-mortem page
Use AWS Systems Manager OpsCenter to automatically generate post-mortem reports and sync them to Confluence using the OpsCenter-Confluence native integration
Configure Incident Manager to send incident summaries to an S3 bucket on resolution, then trigger a Lambda that converts the JSON to Confluence wiki markup and creates the page
Require incident responders to manually export Incident Manager incident reports as PDF and upload them to Confluence after each incident resolution
explain: EventBridge captures Incident Manager lifecycle events including incident resolution. A Lambda function triggered on resolution can call the AWS SSM Incident Manager API to fetch the incident timeline, impact summary, actions taken, and involved contacts, then use the Confluence REST API to create a structured post-mortem page with pre-populated fields. This automates the documentation initiation step. Manual export defeats automation. SSM OpsCenter does not have a native Confluence integration. The S3 bucket approach adds unnecessary intermediate steps when Lambda can call both APIs directly.

q: A company runs an application on EC2 instances in an Auto Scaling group. During maintenance, they need to update the application on all instances without reducing capacity below 80% of current desired count. Rolling updates must happen instance by instance. Which ASG feature automates this?
options: Use ASG Instance Refresh with a minimum healthy percentage of 80%, which replaces instances one at a time (or in batches), ensuring at least 80% of instances are in service throughout the update process
Configure a Blue/Green deployment by creating a new ASG with the updated launch template and using a weighted ALB target group to gradually shift traffic
Use AWS CodeDeploy with an EC2 deployment group configured for rolling deployment with 20% maximum unavailable instances targeting the ASG
Manually suspend the Launch and Terminate processes in the ASG, update instances individually via Systems Manager Run Command, then resume ASG processes
explain: ASG Instance Refresh natively handles rolling replacements with a configurable minimum healthy percentage. Setting 80% ensures at least 80% of the desired count remains healthy throughout the refresh, replacing instances in batches while maintaining availability. Blue/Green with a new ASG is valid but more complex and requires ALB target group weight management. CodeDeploy with EC2 deployment groups works but requires CodeDeploy agent setup and configuration on instances. Manual process suspension defeats automation and is error-prone.

q: A team's CodePipeline includes a CodeBuild stage that builds a Java application using Maven. The Maven repository manager is hosted on-premises. CodeBuild cannot reach the on-premises network. Which solution allows CodeBuild to access the on-premises Maven repository without exposing it to the public internet?
options: Configure CodeBuild to run in a VPC connected to the on-premises network via AWS Direct Connect or Site-to-Site VPN; CodeBuild instances in the VPC can reach the on-premises Maven repository through the private network connection
Use Amazon S3 as a Maven repository cache by running a cron job that syncs the on-premises repository contents to S3 hourly; CodeBuild reads artifacts from S3
Configure an AWS Lambda function as a proxy that forwards Maven requests from CodeBuild to the on-premises repository via an HTTPS tunnel
Migrate the on-premises Maven repository to AWS CodeArtifact and configure CodeArtifact as an upstream proxy; CodeBuild accesses CodeArtifact within the AWS network
explain: Running CodeBuild in a VPC enables the build environment to participate in the private network. With AWS Direct Connect or Site-to-Site VPN establishing connectivity between the VPC and on-premises network, CodeBuild instances can reach on-premises services via private IP addressing — the Maven repository never needs a public endpoint. The VPC configuration specifies subnets and security groups for the CodeBuild project. Migrating to CodeArtifact is a valid long-term solution but requires Maven repository migration effort. A Lambda proxy adds complexity and has payload size limits incompatible with large Maven artifacts. S3 sync introduces a 1-hour staleness window and requires a separate synchronization infrastructure.

q: A DevOps team discovers that developers are creating IAM roles with overly-broad permissions that violate least-privilege policies. The team wants to automatically analyze every new IAM role for excessive permissions and flag violations before the role is used in production. Which AWS service performs this analysis?
options: Enable AWS IAM Access Analyzer to generate findings for IAM roles that grant access to external principals or resources outside the account; configure EventBridge to forward findings to the security team's Slack channel
Enable Amazon GuardDuty with the IAM threat detection module; GuardDuty analyzes IAM role usage patterns and generates findings when roles are used in ways that indicate excessive permissions
Use AWS Security Hub with CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark checks; Security Hub automatically evaluates IAM roles against the benchmark and generates findings for non-compliant role configurations

Use AWS IAM Access Analyzer policy validation to analyze IAM policies during the CodePipeline build stage; Access Analyzer checks policies against AWS best practices and security standards, returning warnings for overly-broad permissions
explain: AWS IAM Access Analyzer policy validation analyzes IAM policy documents against AWS best practices, security standards, and common mistakes. The CheckNoNewAccess and ValidatePolicy APIs return warnings and errors for overly-broad permissions (wildcards in actions, *:* resource patterns, unused conditions). Integrating this into the CodeBuild stage of a CI/CD pipeline — running aws accessanalyzer validate-policy against the IAM policy JSON before deployment — catches violations before the role reaches production. IAM Access Analyzer findings (external access) detect cross-account or public access, not overly-broad permissions for same-account principals. GuardDuty detects active threats from credential usage, not policy design. Security Hub CIS checks verify specific hardening standards but don't perform granular policy analysis for custom role permissions.

q: A team has structured logs in CloudWatch Logs with a field named requestDuration. They want to create a dashboard widget showing the 95th percentile request duration over time as a line graph. Which approach works directly with CloudWatch?
options: Configure CloudWatch Embedded Metrics Format (EMF) in the application to emit requestDuration as a metric, then use p95 statistic in a dashboard widget
Use CloudWatch Logs Insights to run a scheduled query computing pct(requestDuration, 95) hourly and store results in an S3 file referenced by the dashboard
Create a CloudWatch Logs metric filter with a JSON filter extracting requestDuration values and publishing them to a CloudWatch metric, then use the p95 statistic on that metric in a dashboard widget
Both A and C are valid approaches depending on whether logs are already flowing or the application can be modified
explain: Both metric filters and EMF are valid CloudWatch-native approaches to get percentile statistics on a log-derived metric. Metric filters extract the numeric value and publish it to CloudWatch Metrics, where p95 statistic becomes available for graphing. EMF embeds metric directives in log lines so CloudWatch automatically creates the metric. If logs are already flowing without EMF, option A (metric filter) works. If the application can be modified, EMF (option C) is preferable as it is more expressive. Logs Insights scheduled queries do not publish to CloudWatch Metrics natively.

q: A team needs to implement canary deployment monitoring where a Lambda function automatically triggers a rollback if error rates in the new deployment exceed 5% within the first 10 minutes. Which deployment and monitoring configuration achieves this?
options: Configure Lambda provisioned concurrency for the new version and route 10% of traffic while monitoring X-Ray error rates in the console before promoting

Use AWS CodeDeploy Lambda deployment with a Canary10Percent10Minutes deployment configuration, configure a pre-traffic or post-traffic hook Lambda to check CloudWatch metrics, and use CodeDeploy rollback triggers based on CloudWatch alarms monitoring error rate
Deploy the Lambda function with 10% weighted alias traffic to the new version, manually monitor CloudWatch metrics for 10 minutes, and update alias weights if error rate is acceptable
Use Lambda function aliases with traffic shifting from 0% to 100% over 10 minutes using a Step Functions state machine that checks metrics at each step
explain: CodeDeploy's Canary10Percent10Minutes deployment configuration automatically routes 10% of traffic to the new Lambda version and the remaining 90% to the old version. After 10 minutes, it shifts all traffic to the new version if no rollback is triggered. Pre/post-traffic hook Lambdas and CloudWatch alarm-based rollback triggers enable automatic rollback when the error rate alarm fires. Manual monitoring cannot guarantee an automated response within the 10-minute window. Step Functions with metric checks is a valid alternative but more complex to implement than CodeDeploy's built-in deployment configurations. Provisioned concurrency is about cold start latency, not traffic shifting.

q: A company uses ElastiCache Redis for session storage. Their Redis cluster is single-node and a node failure causes application outages. How should they improve Redis availability with automatic failover?
options: Enable ElastiCache Redis cluster mode with 3 shards and no replicas, distributing the keyspace across shards to improve throughput and reduce single-node impact

Convert the single-node Redis cluster to a Redis replication group with one primary and at least one replica in a different AZ, enabling automatic failover so that a replica is promoted to primary within 1-2 minutes of a primary failure
Enable ElastiCache for Redis with backup snapshots every 15 minutes and restore from the latest snapshot when a node failure occurs
Configure ElastiCache Redis Global Datastore to replicate the session data to a secondary region and update the application to read from the nearest region
explain: Redis replication groups with Multi-AZ enabled provide automatic failover. When the primary node fails, ElastiCache promotes a replica to primary automatically within 1-2 minutes, minimizing downtime. The replica must be in a different AZ to protect against AZ failures. Cluster mode with no replicas distributes data across shards but still has single-shard failure risk. Global Datastore provides cross-region replication for DR, not AZ-level HA. Restore from snapshots has a much longer RTO than automatic failover.

q: A company wants to implement infrastructure drift detection. Every 6 hours, they want to compare the current AWS resource configuration against the approved CloudFormation template and alert when any resource has been manually modified outside of CloudFormation. Which solution detects drift with the least custom code?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager Automation runbook AWS-DetectCFNStackDrift on a schedule via State Manager; the runbook handles the async drift detection workflow and publishes results to an SNS topic

Use AWS Config with the cloudformation-stack-drift-detection-check managed rule on a 6-hour evaluation schedule; Config automatically detects drift and generates findings when stack resources have been modified outside CloudFormation

Schedule a Lambda function every 6 hours that calls cloudformation:DetectStackDrift for all stacks, then polls cloudformation:DescribeStackDriftDetectionStatus until complete, retrieves drifted resources via cloudformation:DescribeStackResourceDrifts, and publishes a drift report to an SNS topic

Configure an EventBridge Scheduler rule that triggers a CodePipeline execution every 6 hours with a drift detection CodeBuild action; the pipeline fails if drift is detected, sending a CloudWatch alarm
explain: AWS Systems Manager Automation's AWS-DetectCFNStackDrift runbook encapsulates the full drift detection workflow: initiating detection, polling until complete, retrieving drifted resources, and publishing results — without custom Lambda code. State Manager associations can schedule the runbook on any interval including every 6 hours. AWS Config's cloudformation-stack-drift-detection-check rule exists but triggers on resource configuration changes rather than on a fixed schedule, making it less predictable for scheduled detection. A custom Lambda function implementing the full detection workflow requires writing and maintaining code for async polling. A CodePipeline execution for drift detection is overly complex and conflates CI/CD with operational monitoring.

q: A company's AWS infrastructure is defined entirely in CDK. A developer accidentally ran cdk destroy on a production stack containing an RDS database. AWS Backup had a recovery point from 2 hours before the destroy. The team needs to restore the database and update the CDK stack to manage the restored instance. Which recovery sequence minimizes downtime and maintains CDK control?
options: Use AWS Backup to restore the RDS instance from the recovery point to a new RDS instance; then use cloudformation:ImportResource to import the restored RDS instance under the original logical ID in the CDK/CloudFormation stack, resuming CDK management
Contact AWS Support to reverse the cdk destroy operation; CloudFormation maintains deleted stack history for 90 days and can restore the previous stack state
Restore the RDS instance from the AWS Backup recovery point; update the CDK stack to remove the RDS construct and deploy; then separately manage the restored database outside CDK
Recreate the RDS instance from scratch using the CDK stack; AWS Backup recovery points cannot be used with CDK-managed resources once the CloudFormation stack is deleted
explain: AWS Backup restores the RDS instance from the recovery point as a new DB instance. The cloudformation:ImportResource feature allows importing existing AWS resources (including RDS instances not managed by CloudFormation) into an existing stack under a specified logical ID. After import, the CDK/CloudFormation stack manages the restored instance as if it had been created by CloudFormation — future cdk deploy operations can modify it, and cdk destroy would delete it (with RemovalPolicy.RETAIN providing protection). This sequence minimizes RTO (restore from backup + import) while maintaining infrastructure-as-code governance. Recreating from scratch ignores the Backup recovery point containing 2 hours less data loss. AWS Support cannot reverse cdk destroy operations. Managing the database outside CDK creates ungoverned infrastructure drift.

q: An organization uses multiple AWS accounts and wants to perform centralized CloudTrail log analysis using SQL queries. Logs from all accounts are delivered to a central S3 bucket. Which service enables serverless SQL queries directly against the S3-stored CloudTrail logs?
options: Stream CloudTrail logs from S3 to Amazon OpenSearch using Lambda and query using the OpenSearch Dashboards SQL plugin
Create an Athena table using the CloudTrail partition projection configuration pointing to the central S3 bucket, then query using standard SQL with Athena
Use Amazon EMR with Apache Hive to define an external table over the CloudTrail S3 bucket and submit HiveQL queries for analysis
Configure Amazon Redshift Spectrum to query the CloudTrail S3 bucket by creating an external table in the Redshift cluster's external schema
explain: Amazon Athena is a serverless interactive query service that queries data directly in S3 using standard SQL with no infrastructure to manage. CloudTrail logs have well-documented Athena table schemas with partition projection support, enabling efficient time-range queries without manual partition management. Redshift Spectrum requires a running Redshift cluster with associated costs. EMR with Hive requires cluster management. OpenSearch requires streaming all data from S3 before querying, adding latency and cost.

q: An organization receives a notification that an EC2 instance may have been used to launch a DDoS attack against external targets. The security team needs to immediately stop the attack while preserving evidence for investigation. What is the priority order of actions?
options: First isolate the instance by applying a restrictive security group blocking all outbound traffic except to management hosts; then create EBS snapshots and enable VPC Flow Logs to capture ongoing traffic; then notify AWS Trust & Safety if the attack is confirmed
Contact AWS Support first to block the traffic at the network level, then investigate the instance while it continues running to capture attack patterns
Increase the instance's security group egress rules to rate-limit the attack traffic, then run a full anti-malware scan using AWS Systems Manager while the instance remains live
Immediately terminate the instance to stop the attack, then review CloudTrail logs to understand how the instance was compromised, and restore from a clean AMI
explain: Isolation (not termination) stops the attack while preserving forensic evidence. Applying a restrictive outbound security group immediately stops malicious egress traffic. EBS snapshots preserve disk state for forensic analysis including malware artifacts. VPC Flow Logs capture any remaining connection attempts. Notifying AWS Trust & Safety is required if the attack is confirmed to be originating from the account. Immediate termination destroys volatile memory and prevents forensic analysis. Contacting AWS Support first delays stopping the attack. Rate-limiting through security groups is not a native feature and anti-malware scanning while the instance is active risks evidence contamination.

q: A DevOps engineer must implement health checks for an Auto Scaling group that uses EC2 instances running a web application. The application may pass the EC2 system status check but fail at the application level. Which health check configuration should be used?
options: Use the default EC2 health check type, which monitors instance status checks at the hypervisor level and replaces instances that fail status checks
Enable CloudWatch detailed monitoring on all instances and create an alarm that terminates unhealthy instances when application metrics drop below threshold
Configure an ASG lifecycle hook that runs a health check script on each instance during the InService transition and terminates instances that fail the script

Configure the ASG with ELB health checks by attaching the instances to an ALB target group, where the ALB performs application-level HTTP health checks to a specific endpoint; the ASG replaces instances that fail ALB health checks
explain: ASG ELB health checks delegate health determination to the ALB, which performs application-level HTTP checks (e.g., checking for 200 OK on /health). When an instance fails the ALB health check, the ASG replaces it — even if the underlying EC2 instance passes system status checks. Default EC2 health checks only monitor the hypervisor-level status and miss application failures. Lifecycle hooks run once during instance launch/termination, not continuously. CloudWatch alarms can trigger actions but require custom automation to terminate specific instances.

q: A company is migrating from Jenkins to AWS CodePipeline. Existing Jenkins jobs use a shared library of Groovy scripts for common build functions (version bumping, artifact signing, test reporting). The DevOps team wants to reuse these common functions in CodeBuild without rewriting them in a different language. Which approach best preserves the shared library pattern in CodeBuild?
options: Store the Groovy scripts in a CodeCommit repository; add a pre_build phase step in each CodeBuild buildspec that clones the shared library repository and sources the scripts before running build commands
Rewrite all Groovy shared library functions as bash scripts and store them in an S3 bucket; CodeBuild downloads and executes them in the pre_build phase
Package the Groovy shared library functions as an AWS Lambda layer; CodeBuild actions call the Lambda functions using the AWS CLI during the build phase
Package the common build logic as a custom CodeBuild environment image that includes the Groovy runtime and pre-loaded scripts; projects inherit the shared functions by using this base image
explain: A custom CodeBuild environment image (Docker image stored in ECR) that includes the Groovy runtime, all shared library scripts, and any required tools provides a consistent, versioned, centrally managed environment. All CodeBuild projects using this image have immediate access to the shared functions without cloning external repositories. Updates to shared functions require rebuilding the base image — which itself can be automated via a CodePipeline. Cloning a shared library repo in pre_build works but adds latency, requires network access to CodeCommit, and creates a runtime dependency on repository availability. Lambda layers are not invokable as functions from CodeBuild build commands directly. Bash rewrites lose the existing Groovy investment and introduce a rewrite risk.

q: A company wants to standardize all EC2 instance configurations using AWS Systems Manager. They need each instance to join the corporate Active Directory domain on first boot, install required security agents, and configure specific OS hardening settings. This must happen automatically for every new instance without manual intervention. Which SSM mechanism applies all three configurations automatically on first boot?
options: Use SSM Quick Setup with a custom configuration that installs agents, joins the domain, and applies OS hardening; Quick Setup automatically creates all required State Manager associations and IAM roles
Configure a State Manager association targeting EC2 instances by tag with a schedule of 'once on boot' using cron(0 0 ? * MON-SUN *); the document runs all three configuration steps sequentially

Configure SSM documents with onInstanceLaunch triggers in the association; SSM detects new instance launches via CloudWatch Events and runs the configuration sequence automatically on first registration
Use EC2 user data to run a PowerShell/bash script that calls SSM Run Command for each configuration step; user data provides the trigger for first-boot execution
explain: AWS Systems Manager Quick Setup provides a simplified interface for applying common configuration patterns to EC2 instances.
