For custom configuration, Quick Setup creates the required State Manager associations, IAM instance profile roles, and CloudWatch alarms automatically. The associations can be configured to run on first registration (immediately when the instance registers with SSM) rather than on a schedule, ensuring domain join, agent installation, and hardening occur at first boot. EC2 user data provides a first-boot trigger but doesn't integrate with SSM State Manager's drift detection or compliance reporting. State Manager associations with cron schedules run periodically, not specifically on first boot. SSM documents don't have native onInstanceLaunch trigger types; EventBridge rules for SSM registration events can trigger associations but require custom EventBridge/Lambda configuration.

q: A company wants to monitor their AWS Lambda functions for cold start duration and memory usage trends. They need per-function breakdowns visible in a single dashboard. Which is the most direct approach with minimal custom code?
options: Enable Lambda Insights by adding the CloudWatch Lambda Insights extension layer to functions, which automatically publishes enhanced metrics including cold start duration, memory usage, and init duration to CloudWatch
Parse Lambda function CloudWatch Logs REPORT lines with a metric filter extracting Init Duration and Max Memory Used fields for each function
Configure CloudWatch Embedded Metrics Format in each Lambda function to emit custom cold start and memory metrics in structured log lines
Instrument each Lambda function with X-Ray active tracing and use the X-Ray service map to identify initialization time as subsegment duration
explain: CloudWatch Lambda Insights is an add-on layer that runs as a Lambda extension alongside the function. It automatically captures and publishes enhanced performance metrics including cold start duration, memory used vs provisioned, and CPU time without code changes. Log parsing with metric filters requires writing and testing regex for REPORT line formats. X-Ray shows trace timing but does not provide memory utilization metrics. EMF requires code changes in every function.

q: A team uses AWS Systems Manager to manage their EC2 fleet. They want to automate the response to a known software vulnerability by patching all affected instances within 2 hours of the vulnerability being published. Which SSM feature automates this?
options: Create an SSM Patch Manager patch baseline with the vulnerability's CVE, configure a patch group for affected instances, and trigger an on-demand patch operation using SSM Automation with the AWS-RunPatchBaseline document targeting the affected patch group
Use SSM Run Command to manually run the patch installation command on each affected instance by targeting instance IDs identified from the inventory
Schedule an SSM Maintenance Window that runs weekly patch scans and applies all available security patches, relying on the next scheduled window to remediate new vulnerabilities
Configure AWS Inspector to scan for the vulnerability and auto-generate SSM patch commands in the Inspector finding for manual execution by the operations team
explain: SSM Patch Manager with on-demand patching allows immediate remediation outside of Maintenance Windows. Creating a patch baseline targeting the specific CVE and triggering AWS-RunPatchBaseline via SSM Automation against the affected patch group achieves automated patching within the 2-hour window. Maintenance Windows enforce scheduled patching which cannot meet the 2-hour on-demand requirement for a newly published CVE. Manual Run Command targeting individual instances does not scale to large fleets. Inspector detects vulnerabilities but does not automate remediation — findings still require manual action.

q: A company needs to implement a load balancing solution that supports both HTTP/2 and WebSocket connections for a real-time collaboration application, while providing native SSL termination and host-based routing to multiple backend services. Which load balancer type should they use?
options: Classic Load Balancer (CLB), which supports HTTP/HTTPS and TCP protocols including WebSocket upgrades with connection-level load balancing
Application Load Balancer (ALB), which natively supports HTTP/2, WebSockets, SSL termination, and content-based routing including host-based and path-based routing rules
Network Load Balancer (NLB), which operates at Layer 4 and provides ultra-low latency TCP connections supporting WebSockets and HTTP/2 pass-through
Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB), which supports transparent network traffic inspection and can handle HTTP/2 and WebSocket traffic at the network layer
explain: ALB operates at Layer 7 (application layer) and natively supports HTTP/2 (including server push), WebSocket upgrades (HTTP Upgrade header), SSL/TLS termination with ACM certificates, and advanced content-based routing including host headers, paths, query strings, and HTTP methods. NLB operates at Layer 4 and provides pass-through for WebSockets but does not perform SSL termination natively or content-based routing. CLB is legacy and lacks host-based routing and HTTP/2 support. GWLB is designed for third-party virtual appliance integration, not application load balancing.

q: A DevOps team has 50 microservices each deploying independently via CodePipeline. They need a single dashboard view showing the current deployment status of all 50 services simultaneously — which services are deploying, which are stable, and which have failed deployments in the last 24 hours. Which AWS service provides this without building a custom application?
options: Amazon CloudWatch Dashboards with CodePipeline metric widgets displaying pipeline execution counts and failure rates per pipeline
Use Amazon DevOps Guru to monitor all pipelines and generate an operational summary with anomaly detection for pipelines that deviate from normal execution patterns
AWS CodePipeline console's pipeline list view, which shows the current state of all pipelines and allows filtering by status
Use Amazon CloudWatch Application Signals with CodePipeline as the monitored service, configuring SLOs for each pipeline's success rate
explain: The AWS CodePipeline console lists all pipelines in an account/region with their current execution status (In Progress, Succeeded, Failed) and the time of the last execution. Filtering by status and sorting by recent activity provides an immediate operational view of all 50 pipelines without any configuration. CloudWatch Dashboards require manually adding each pipeline as a metric source — with 50 services this is configuration-heavy and doesn't show real-time execution status in the same way as the console. DevOps Guru detects anomalies but doesn't provide a deployment status dashboard. CloudWatch Application Signals is designed for application-level observability, not pipeline execution monitoring.

q: A team uses CloudFormation to deploy a multi-tier application. The networking tier (VPC, subnets, route tables) rarely changes. The application tier deploys frequently. Currently they're in one 450-resource stack causing slow deployments. They want to separate the stacks while allowing the application tier to reference networking outputs. Which cross-stack reference pattern is most maintainable?
options: Deploy both stacks from a parent stack using nested stacks; reference networking outputs in the application nested stack using GetAtt on the networking nested stack resource

Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to pass values between stacks; the networking stack writes VPC ID and subnet IDs to Parameter Store parameters on each deploy; the application stack reads them using the ssm: dynamic reference pattern at deploy time
Use hardcoded values from the networking stack in the application stack template; networking resources rarely change so static values provide the simplest cross-stack reference approach

Use CloudFormation exports (Outputs with Export Name) from the networking stack; import values in the application stack using Fn::ImportValue; this creates a hard dependency preventing the networking stack from being deleted while the application stack imports its values
explain: SSM Parameter Store as a cross-stack reference mechanism provides the best decoupling. The networking stack writes its outputs (VPC ID, subnet IDs, route table IDs) to SSM parameters as part of the deployment. The application stack reads these values using the {{resolve:ssm:/network/vpc-id}} dynamic reference pattern, fetching the current value from SSM at deployment time. This decouples the stacks completely — the networking stack can be deployed or updated independently without affecting the application stack's ability to reference its outputs. Unlike CloudFormation exports, SSM references don't create hard CloudFormation dependencies that prevent stack deletion. Nested stacks re-couple the lifecycle. Hardcoded values break when networking resources are ever recreated or updated.

q: A DevOps team needs to detect when an EC2 Auto Scaling group scales out more than 3 times within 10 minutes, which may indicate a runaway scaling event. CloudWatch metrics show DesiredCapacity changes. Which solution detects this pattern?
options: Create a CloudWatch composite alarm combining three individual scale-out event alarms with AND logic to detect three simultaneous scaling events

Create an EventBridge rule matching EC2 Auto Scaling events for SuccessfulScaleOut, with a Step Functions state machine that counts events in a 10-minute window using DynamoDB and triggers an SNS alert when count exceeds 3
Set a CloudWatch Alarm on the DesiredCapacity metric with threshold 3 and evaluation period 10 minutes to alarm when DesiredCapacity increases more than 3 units
Configure CloudWatch Contributor Insights on Auto Scaling group activity logs to track scale-out frequency and alert when the contributor count exceeds 3
explain: Counting discrete events within a time window requires stateful logic that CloudWatch alarms cannot provide natively — alarms evaluate metric values, not event counts. An EventBridge rule captures each scale-out event, and a Step Functions workflow uses DynamoDB to atomically increment a counter with a TTL-based 10-minute window, triggering SNS when the count exceeds 3. Alarming on DesiredCapacity threshold detects absolute capacity, not the rate of scale-out events. Contributor Insights tracks top-N contributors in logs, not event counts. Composite alarms require pre-existing child alarms, not event counting.

q: A team needs to implement automatic tagging of all newly created AWS resources with the creator's IAM identity for cost allocation and accountability. Resources created without proper tags should trigger an alert. Which EventBridge-based architecture achieves automatic tagging?
options: Use AWS Resource Groups Tagging API on a weekly schedule to find untagged resources and apply generic tags based on account ID and region
Configure AWS Organizations tag policies to enforce required tags on all resources and block resource creation if required tags are missing

Create an EventBridge rule matching specific resource creation API events (RunInstances, CreateBucket, etc.), invoke a Lambda function that extracts the userIdentity from the CloudTrail event detail and applies tags with creator identity, and use Config rules to alert on untagged resources
Enable AWS Config managed rule required-tags on all resource types and configure manual remediation notifications to the resource creator via email
explain: EventBridge captures resource creation events from CloudTrail. Lambda extracts the userIdentity (ARN, username, or assumed role) from the event and calls the appropriate tagging API (ec2:CreateTags, s3:PutBucketTagging, etc.) immediately after creation. Config rules provide ongoing compliance monitoring for untagged resources with alerting. Tag policies in Organizations can enforce tags but they require resources to be created with the tags — they do not retroactively tag existing resources. Weekly tagging misses the real-time requirement. Config required-tags with manual notifications is reactive and does not auto-tag.

q: A company deploys Lambda functions behind API Gateway and needs to ensure that a sudden spike of 10,000 concurrent requests does not cause all Lambda invocations to fail due to account-level concurrency limits. Other Lambda functions in the account must also remain available. How should they configure this?
options: Set reserved concurrency on the API Gateway Lambda functions to a specific limit (e.g., 5000) to cap their consumption, preventing them from exhausting the account-level limit and ensuring other functions retain their share
Enable Lambda Provisioned Concurrency set to 10,000 to pre-warm execution environments and ensure all 10,000 concurrent requests can be handled without cold starts
Deploy Lambda functions in multiple AWS accounts and use API Gateway as a proxy to distribute requests across accounts to bypass per-account concurrency limits
Configure API Gateway throttling limits at the account and stage level to cap the incoming request rate, preventing Lambda from receiving more requests than the concurrency limit
explain: Reserved concurrency serves two purposes: it guarantees a minimum concurrency for the function and caps its maximum concurrency to protect other functions. Setting 5000 reserved concurrency ensures the API Lambda cannot consume more than 5000 concurrent executions, leaving at least 5000 for other functions (assuming a 10,000 account default). Provisioned concurrency pre-warms environments but does not cap consumption or protect other functions. API Gateway throttling controls request rate but doesn't solve concurrency distribution among functions. Multi-account distribution adds complexity without a native AWS solution.

q: A team uses AWS CodeCommit with branch protection rules requiring two approvals before merging to main. A developer bypassed the branch protection by pushing directly to main using their IAM credentials. The security team wants to prevent direct pushes to protected branches using IAM policies rather than relying solely on CodeCommit approval rules. Which IAM condition prevents direct pushes to the main branch?
options: Attach an IAM policy to developer roles with a Deny on codecommit:GitPush with the condition StringEquals: codecommit:References: refs/heads/main
Use AWS Organizations Tag Policies to tag the main branch as protected; CodeCommit enforces push restrictions based on branch tag policies
Attach an SCP to the developer accounts with a Deny on codecommit:CreateBranch to prevent creation of branches that bypass protection rules
Configure a CodeCommit trigger that fires a Lambda function on push events to main; the Lambda reverts any direct pushes by calling codecommit:MergeBranchesBySquash to undo the commit
explain: IAM policies support the codecommit:References condition key, which matches the git reference being modified in a push operation. A Deny policy with StringEquals codecommit:References matching refs/heads/main prevents any IAM principal with this policy from pushing directly to the main branch via git push, the CLI, or the SDK. This is enforced at the AWS API layer, making it impossible to bypass unlike CodeCommit approval rules which only apply to pull request merges. SCPs operate at account level and codecommit:CreateBranch controls branch creation, not push operations. Lambda reversion is reactive rather than preventive and introduces a window of exposure. Tag Policies enforce resource tagging standards, not repository operation permissions.

q: A company's DevOps team needs to track every change made to production AWS resources, including who made the change, when, from what IP address, and what the resource looked like before and after the change. This audit trail must be tamper-proof and retained for 7 years. Which combination of AWS services satisfies all requirements?
options: Enable AWS Security Hub with all security standards enabled; Security Hub's findings archive provides a 7-year audit trail of all resource changes and access events across the account
Use Amazon CloudWatch Events to capture all AWS API calls and store them in a CloudWatch Log Group with a 2557-day (7-year) retention policy; enable Config for resource state tracking

Enable AWS CloudTrail with a multi-region trail delivering to S3 with Object Lock in compliance mode (7-year retention) and log file integrity validation; enable AWS Config to record resource configuration history showing before/after states on every resource change
Enable CloudTrail in the primary region only with S3 lifecycle policies for 7-year retention; use VPC Flow Logs for network-level change tracking to supplement the CloudTrail API logs
explain: This requirement needs two complementary services. CloudTrail provides the who/when/IP audit trail: every AWS API call is recorded with the calling identity, timestamp, source IP, and full request/response details. Multi-region coverage captures global service calls (IAM, Route 53). S3 Object Lock in compliance mode (7-year retention) creates an immutable audit log that cannot be deleted or overwritten even by account administrators. Log file integrity validation uses SHA-256 hash chains to detect any tampering. AWS Config records the complete before/after configuration state of every resource on each change, satisfying the 'what did the resource look like' requirement. Single-region CloudTrail misses global service events. CloudWatch Events doesn't record full API request/response details. Security Hub aggregates compliance findings, not resource change history.

q: An organization needs to verify that CloudWatch Logs log groups in their production account are not publicly accessible and are encrypted with a KMS customer-managed key. They want continuous compliance monitoring. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Configure CloudTrail data events for CloudWatch Logs API calls and create a CloudWatch Metric Filter alerting when log groups are created without KMS encryption
Run a daily AWS CLI script in a cron job that calls describe-log-groups and checks the kmsKeyId field, emailing a report of unencrypted log groups
Enable AWS Security Hub with the AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standard, which includes controls for CloudWatch Logs encryption checks

Create AWS Config custom rules using Lambda evaluators that check each log group for KMS encryption and access policy, with auto-remediation configured to call AssociateKmsKey for non-compliant log groups
explain: AWS Config custom rules provide continuous, event-driven compliance evaluation triggered on configuration changes, not just daily. Auto-remediation using SSM Automation documents can automatically call AssociateKmsKey to encrypt non-compliant log groups, providing both detection and remediation. Daily CLI scripts are reactive and miss violations between runs. Security Hub's FSBP standard has limited CloudWatch Logs controls and does not cover all encryption requirements. CloudTrail metric filters only catch new log group creation, not existing unencrypted groups.

q: A company needs to implement a mechanism where developers can request temporary elevated access to production resources during incidents, and access is automatically revoked after 4 hours. Which approach uses least-privilege principles with automatic expiration?
options: Create a permanent IAM role with production access and provide developers with role credentials during incidents, manually removing the role after each incident

Use AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO) to create a permission set for incident access, configure temporary assignment of the permission set to the requesting developer for a 4-hour session, which is automatically revoked when the session expires
Add developers to a production IAM group during incidents and schedule a Lambda function to remove them from the group after 4 hours using a time-delayed EventBridge rule
Configure IAM user temporary security credentials by calling STS GetFederationToken with a 4-hour duration, providing the credentials to developers for the incident duration
explain: IAM Identity Center supports just-in-time access with temporary account assignments that automatically expire after the configured duration. This is the recommended AWS approach for temporary elevated access with automatic revocation and full audit trail in CloudTrail. A permanent role with manual removal depends on human follow-through and creates risk of forgotten access. STS GetFederationToken credentials expire but require the caller to manage and distribute credentials securely. Lambda with time-delayed revocation has a risk of failure if the Lambda itself fails or the EventBridge rule is misconfigured.

q: A team needs to configure Route 53 to serve traffic from us-east-1 normally, but automatically route 100% of traffic to eu-west-1 when the us-east-1 ALB health check fails. Both regions serve the same application. Which Route 53 routing policy achieves this?
options: Configure Route 53 geolocation routing to send all North America traffic to us-east-1 and European traffic to eu-west-1, with health checks on each record
Configure Route 53 weighted routing with 100 weight for us-east-1 and 0 weight for eu-west-1, and manually adjust weights during a regional failure
Configure Route 53 latency-based routing with health checks on both regions; users are routed to eu-west-1 when us-east-1 has high latency due to the failure

Configure Route 53 failover routing with the us-east-1 ALB as the primary record with an associated health check, and the eu-west-1 ALB as the secondary record; Route 53 automatically routes all traffic to the secondary when the primary health check fails
explain: Route 53 failover routing is designed exactly for this active-passive DR pattern. The primary record has a health check; when it fails, Route 53 immediately starts responding with the secondary record. This routes 100% of traffic to eu-west-1 automatically. Weighted routing requires manual weight adjustment during failures. Latency-based routing chooses the lowest-latency healthy endpoint — both regions would still serve traffic if both are healthy, and during a failure, unhealthy endpoints are automatically excluded, but it is not designed as an active-passive failover policy. Geolocation routing serves users based on location regardless of health.

q: A company runs 200 CodePipeline pipelines across 15 AWS accounts in an organization. The security team requires a monthly report listing all pipelines that had at least one FAILED execution in the past 30 days, including the failure reason and the AWS account. Which approach produces this report with the least custom infrastructure?
options: Build a Lambda function that iterates all 15 accounts using STS AssumeRole, calls codepipeline:ListPipelineExecutions for each pipeline, filters for FAILED status within 30 days, and writes the report to S3
Configure AWS Config aggregator across all 15 accounts; use the Config advanced query to select all CodePipeline pipeline resources with failedExecutions > 0 in the last 30 days
Enable AWS CloudTrail in all accounts with a central S3 bucket; use Athena to query CloudTrail logs for CodePipeline StageExecution events with status FAILED in the past 30 days

Use Amazon EventBridge in each account to forward CodePipeline FAILED events to a central EventBridge event bus in the security account; use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query aggregated pipeline failure events over 30 days
explain: Amazon EventBridge supports cross-account event forwarding via event bus resource policies. Each member account creates an EventBridge rule matching CodePipeline execution state change events with status FAILED and routes them to a central event bus in the security account. The central account's rule sends failures to CloudWatch Logs. CloudWatch Logs Insights queries the aggregated log group for the past 30 days, producing a report with pipeline name, account, failure reason, and timestamp — without custom Lambda infrastructure. A Lambda report generator requires cross-account role setup, pagination through all pipelines, and management of the Lambda execution. CloudTrail Athena querying works but requires table partitioning across 15 accounts. AWS Config doesn't track pipeline execution history.

q: A DevOps team uses Ansible for EC2 configuration management. They want to continue using Ansible but integrate it with AWS Systems Manager so they can run Ansible playbooks on EC2 instances without maintaining a bastion host or opening inbound SSH ports. Which SSM feature enables this?
options: SSM Automation with the AWS-ApplyAnsiblePlaybooks runbook; Automation orchestrates Ansible execution across EC2 instances using the SSM agent as the execution transport
SSM Run Command using the AWS-RunAnsiblePlaybook document; Run Command executes Ansible playbooks stored in S3 on target EC2 instances through the SSM agent without requiring SSH or a bastion host
SSM Session Manager with port forwarding to tunnel SSH connections through the SSM agent; this maintains SSH security without a bastion host while allowing Ansible SSH connections
Install the Ansible Tower agent on EC2 instances; Tower communicates with instances through the SSM agent channel instead of direct SSH
explain: AWS Systems Manager Run Command includes the AWS-RunAnsiblePlaybook document that downloads an Ansible playbook from S3, installs Ansible if not present, and executes the playbook locally on the target instance through the SSM agent. The SSM agent uses an outbound HTTPS connection to the SSM service, requiring no inbound firewall rules or SSH open ports. Multiple instances can be targeted simultaneously using tags or resource groups, and Run Command provides execution status and output for each instance. Session Manager port forwarding still requires SSH infrastructure on the instance. SSM Automation with AWS-ApplyAnsiblePlaybooks exists and is valid, but Run Command is the simpler, more direct mechanism for Ansible playbook execution. Ansible Tower has its own agent mechanism separate from SSM.

q: A team is troubleshooting intermittent 5xx errors from their Application Load Balancer. They want to correlate ALB access logs with application logs in CloudWatch Logs using the X-Amzn-Trace-Id header. Which approach enables end-to-end correlation?
options: Configure ALB to write access logs directly to CloudWatch Logs and use a Logs Insights join query to correlate ALB and application log groups by timestamp

Store ALB access logs in S3 with Athena queries using the trace_id field, and configure the application to log the X-Amzn-Trace-Id header value in CloudWatch Logs, enabling correlation by trace ID across both systems
Enable VPC Flow Logs and correlate connection-level data with application logs using source port numbers to trace individual requests
Enable X-Ray active tracing on the ALB and configure the application to use X-Ray SDK, then view the complete trace including ALB timing in the X-Ray console
explain: ALB logs include the trace_id field from the X-Amzn-Trace-Id header. When the application logs this same header value (e.g., extracted in a middleware layer), you can query both Athena (for ALB logs) and CloudWatch Logs Insights (for application logs) using the trace ID as the correlation key. ALB does not support X-Ray active tracing as a first-class feature. CloudWatch Logs Insights cannot perform joins between log groups. VPC Flow Logs contain network-level TCP data without application-layer request identifiers.

q: A security team detects that a large amount of data is being exfiltrated from an RDS PostgreSQL database to an unexpected external IP address. The database is in a private VPC. What immediate containment action and investigation step should be taken?
options: Immediately modify the RDS security group to remove all outbound rules to the external IP, enable RDS Enhanced Monitoring and Performance Insights to capture in-flight query data, then review VPC Flow Logs and RDS PostgreSQL logs for the exfiltration queries
Take an RDS snapshot immediately and restore it in an isolated VPC for forensic analysis while keeping the original database running
Enable RDS audit logging and wait for 24 hours of additional logs to accumulate before analyzing the exfiltration pattern and taking containment action
Stop the RDS instance immediately to halt the exfiltration and contact AWS Support to retrieve the database transaction logs from the stopped instance
explain: RDS security groups control network access to the database endpoint. Removing the outbound rule to the external IP stops the exfiltration while leaving the database operational. RDS Enhanced Monitoring captures OS-level metrics and Performance Insights shows current and recent SQL queries, helping identify the exfiltration queries. VPC Flow Logs show network-level connection data and RDS PostgreSQL logs show query-level activity. Taking a snapshot while the database continues running loses the opportunity to stop ongoing exfiltration. Waiting 24 hours for more logs allows continued data loss. Stopping the RDS instance disrupts legitimate users and transaction logs are managed by AWS, not directly retrievable.

q: A team is deploying a stateless API on EC2 behind an ALB. They need to ensure that during an AZ failure, the application continues to serve traffic without manual intervention. The ASG currently has a desired capacity of 6 instances with 2 per AZ across 3 AZs. What configuration change is needed?
options: Set the ASG minimum capacity to 6 and enable AZ rebalancing so that if one AZ fails, the ASG launches replacement instances in the remaining healthy AZs immediately
Use ALB multi-value answer routing to distribute requests across all healthy instances in unaffected AZs without changing the ASG configuration

Enable ALB cross-zone load balancing and configure the ASG with 9 instances (3 per AZ) so that losing one AZ leaves 6 instances across 2 AZs — enough to handle expected load — and the ASG rebalances automatically
Configure dedicated EC2 instances in each AZ to ensure instance placement is guaranteed and not affected by AZ capacity constraints during a failure
explain: Setting ASG minimum capacity to 6 ensures that when one AZ fails and 2 instances are lost, the ASG automatically launches 2 replacement instances in the remaining healthy AZs to restore to minimum capacity. AZ rebalancing is enabled by default and actively redistributes instances when AZ capacity is restored. Cross-zone load balancing and larger desired counts help handle load distribution, but the core requirement is ASG minimum capacity enforcement. Dedicated instances guarantee physical hardware but do not address AZ-level failures. ALB multi-value answer is a DNS feature, not a load balancer configuration.

q: A team's CodePipeline deploys using CloudFormation change sets. Before executing the change set, a reviewer must inspect what resources will be modified. The team wants the change set diff to be automatically posted to the pull request comments so reviewers can see infrastructure changes alongside code changes. Which architecture achieves this?
options: Add a Manual Approval action with the change set ARN in the message; reviewers must navigate to the CloudFormation console to view the diff before approving
Configure CloudFormation to automatically publish change set summaries to SNS, then create an SNS subscription that posts to the pull request API
Add a CodeBuild action after the change set creation step that calls cloudformation:DescribeChangeSet, formats the changes as markdown, and posts to the pull request via the CodeCommit or GitHub API
Use AWS CodeGuru Reviewer to analyze CloudFormation template changes and post automated recommendations to the pull request
explain: A CodeBuild action positioned between the CloudFormation change set creation action and the change set execution action can call cloudformation:DescribeChangeSet to retrieve the full list of resource actions (Add, Modify, Remove) and their properties. The CodeBuild action formats this into a readable markdown diff and posts it to the pull request using the CodeCommit UpdatePullRequestDescription API or GitHub pull request comment API. This provides reviewers with the infrastructure diff directly in the code review context. CloudFormation has no native SNS integration for change set summaries. CodeGuru Reviewer analyzes application code quality, not CloudFormation templates. A Manual Approval action with only an ARN requires reviewers to navigate away from the PR to the CloudFormation console.

q: A company uses AWS CloudFormation StackSets to deploy a security baseline (CloudTrail, Config, GuardDuty) across all accounts in their AWS Organization. After enabling StackSets with automatic deployment for a target OU, newly created accounts in that OU don't receive the baseline stack for 2-3 hours. What causes this delay?
options: AWS CloudFormation StackSets has a built-in 2-hour cooling period after new account creation before it checks for auto-deployment targets to prevent premature deployments to accounts still being provisioned
AWS Config must complete an initial configuration recording cycle in the new account before StackSets can deploy Config-related resources, creating the observed delay
The StackSets auto-deployment uses AWS Control Tower's account vending machine, which has a 2-3 hour provisioning queue during business hours

AWS Organizations has an eventual consistency propagation delay of up to 2-3 hours before StackSets auto-deployment detects a new account in the target OU; the detection event is eventually received and triggers the deployment
explain: AWS CloudFormation StackSets auto-deployment for Organizations integration relies on Organizations event notifications to detect new accounts in target OUs. Due to eventual consistency in the Organizations and StackSets integration, account creation events may take up to 2-3 hours to propagate before StackSets initiates the automatic stack instance deployment. This is a known behavior. Workarounds include deploying the baseline stack immediately after account creation via the StackSets API in the account provisioning pipeline (e.g., in a Control Tower Account Factory customization or a Lambda triggered by the CreateAccountResult event). There is no 2-hour cooling period — StackSets deploys as soon as it receives the event. Control Tower's account vending machine is optional. Config recording doesn't block StackSets deployment of Config resources.

q: A company has a microservices application where each service is a separate AWS account. They need a unified view of service health using CloudWatch dashboards. Cross-account metric sharing has been enabled. Which dashboard feature allows pulling metrics from multiple accounts into one dashboard?
options: Use CloudWatch cross-account cross-region dashboards by adding widgets that reference metrics with account and region selectors after enabling cross-account observability sharing
Deploy a CloudFormation StackSet that creates identical CloudWatch dashboards in each account and use Route 53 weighted routing to load-balance the dashboard URL
Use CloudWatch metric streams to push all source account metrics to a central Amazon Managed Prometheus workspace, then build Grafana dashboards
Create a central Lambda that calls GetMetricData across all source accounts using assumed roles and writes aggregated metrics to the monitoring account's custom namespace
explain: After enabling CloudWatch cross-account observability, monitoring account dashboards can add widgets referencing metrics from source accounts using an account selector. The dashboard builder in the CloudWatch console allows switching the account context per widget, enabling a unified view without data duplication. A central Lambda with assumed roles adds custom engineering overhead. Prometheus and Grafana require additional infrastructure. StackSets create per-account dashboards, not a unified view.

q: A company uses AWS CloudFormation for all infrastructure deployments. After a failed deployment leaves resources in an inconsistent state, the team needs to detect and remediate configuration drift between the desired CloudFormation state and actual resource configuration. Which approach detects and corrects drift?
options: Manually compare the AWS Console configuration of each resource to the CloudFormation template parameters and update resources individually that show differences
Delete and redeploy the entire CloudFormation stack from scratch to ensure all resources match the template configuration
Use AWS Config to compare current resource configurations against the last known CloudFormation deployment and generate a compliance report of drifted resources

Run CloudFormation drift detection on the affected stack to identify resources whose actual configuration differs from the template, then perform a stack update to reconcile the configuration back to the desired state defined in the template
explain: CloudFormation drift detection natively compares the actual resource configuration in AWS with the expected configuration from the stack template. It identifies which resources have drifted and which properties differ. A subsequent CloudFormation stack update with the same template (or a corrected one) resets drifted resources back to the desired state through normal CloudFormation resource update operations. Deleting and redeploying risks data loss for stateful resources like RDS. Manual comparison is error-prone and does not scale. AWS Config tracks configuration history but does not have native awareness of CloudFormation template definitions for drift comparison.

q: A company wants to test the resilience of their multi-AZ application by simulating an AZ failure without affecting production traffic. Which approach safely simulates an AZ failure for testing?
options: Use AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS) to run a chaos experiment that terminates EC2 instances in a specific AZ or injects network disruptions, with a stop condition based on CloudWatch alarms to automatically halt if real impact is detected
Update the security groups of instances in one AZ to deny all inbound traffic, simulating network-level AZ isolation for the test period
Reduce the ASG desired capacity by the number of instances in one AZ to simulate reduced capacity and observe scaling behavior
Manually terminate all EC2 instances in one AZ in the production environment during off-peak hours to observe how the application responds to AZ failure
explain: AWS Fault Injection Simulator (FIS) is the AWS-native chaos engineering service designed for controlled resilience testing. It supports EC2 actions like StopInstances with AZ-targeting conditions, and critically, supports stop conditions based on CloudWatch alarms that automatically terminate the experiment if real service impact is detected. This makes it safe to run in production. Manual instance termination is risky without automated rollback. Reducing ASG desired capacity tests scaling but not actual AZ failure behavior. Security group modifications affect traffic but don't simulate instance or AZ failure patterns accurately.

q: A team deploys a microservice to AWS Lambda using a CodePipeline. After production deployments, they observe that some Lambda function aliases don't get updated with the new version ARN because the update_alias API call in their CodeBuild deploy script occasionally times out under high concurrency. Which deployment approach eliminates the race condition and makes alias updates atomic?
options: Add retry logic with exponential backoff to the update_alias API call in the CodeBuild deploy script; retries handle transient API timeouts without architectural changes
Implement a Step Functions workflow that retries the update_alias call up to 5 times with exponential backoff; Step Functions handles the retry state without CodeBuild
Use AWS SAM with AutoPublishAlias in the function configuration; SAM handles version publication and alias update atomically as part of the CloudFormation stack update, eliminating the race condition
Increase the CodeBuild compute type to large to provide more CPU for concurrent API calls, reducing the likelihood of timeout collisions
explain: AWS SAM's AutoPublishAlias property instructs SAM to automatically publish a new Lambda version on each deployment and update the specified alias to point to the new version. This occurs within the CloudFormation deployment as a single atomic operation — CloudFormation manages the version publication and alias update sequentially, eliminating the race condition inherent in parallel CodeBuild API calls. CloudFormation provides rollback if the alias update fails. Adding retry logic to the deploy script treats the symptom without fixing the root cause — high concurrency still creates race conditions. Step Functions retry logic also treats the symptom. Compute type affects CPU/RAM, not API call serialization or race conditions.

q: A DevOps engineer needs to update a configuration value (API endpoint URL) across 200 EC2 instances simultaneously without restarting the application. The configuration file is /etc/myapp/config.ini on each instance. Which SSM approach updates all instances in parallel with results tracking?
options: Use SSM Run Command with the AWS-RunShellScript document targeting all 200 instances simultaneously using a resource group or tag filter; the command replaces the configuration value using sed, and Run Command provides per-instance success/failure status
Use SSM State Manager to create a new association that deploys the updated configuration file; State Manager automatically detects which instances need the update and applies it in rolling fashion

Use SSM Parameter Store to update the parameter containing the API endpoint URL; the application reads the parameter on each request, automatically picking up the new value without manual file updates
Use SSM Patch Manager with a custom patch baseline that includes the configuration file update as a patch; Patch Manager applies the 'patch' to all instances during the next maintenance window
explain: SSM Run Command with AWS-RunShellScript targets all 200 instances simultaneously (or with configurable concurrency limits using MaxConcurrency and MaxErrors parameters) and executes the sed command to update /etc/myapp/config.ini on each instance. Run Command returns per-instance execution results in real time, and the results can be sent to S3 or CloudWatch Logs for audit. The MaxConcurrency parameter controls parallel execution (e.g., 50% concurrently) and MaxErrors sets the failure threshold. State Manager applies configuration continuously on a schedule rather than as a one-time immediate update. Patch Manager manages OS patches, not application configuration files. Using Parameter Store requires application code changes to read from SSM on each request rather than from a local config file.

q: An application's logs show a correlation between high GC pause durations in JVM logs and application latency spikes. The team wants to create a CloudWatch metric from the GC pause duration logged in milliseconds in CloudWatch Logs. The log format is: 'GC pause duration: 823ms'. Which metric filter pattern extracts the numeric value?
options: Use the pattern 'GC pause duration' without value extraction and count occurrences as a proxy for GC frequency rather than duration
Create a metric filter with pattern { $.gcPause > 0 } assuming the application logs structured JSON with a gcPause field
Use filter pattern [prefix='GC pause duration:', value] with metric value set to $value, extracting the number before 'ms' using a pattern group
Configure a CloudWatch Logs subscription filter to send all logs to a Lambda that parses the GC pause value with regex and calls PutMetricData
explain: CloudWatch Logs metric filters support space-delimited patterns using bracket notation [prefix, value] where $value captures the numeric portion from the matched log line. The extracted value can be used as the metric value (with appropriate post-processing for the 'ms' suffix). For plain-text logs, the space-delimited pattern is the correct approach. JSON pattern { $.gcPause > 0 } requires the application to log structured JSON. Counting occurrences loses duration information. Lambda-based parsing works but adds operational complexity for a task metric filters can handle.

q: A team wants to implement automated evidence collection for security incidents. When a GuardDuty finding of HIGH or CRITICAL severity is generated, the following evidence must be collected automatically: EC2 instance metadata, security group rules, VPC Flow Logs for the past hour, and IAM credential report. Which approach collects all this evidence without manual intervention?
options: Enable AWS Security Hub to aggregate GuardDuty findings and configure Security Hub automated response playbooks to collect EC2 and network evidence automatically
Require the security analyst on-call to run a pre-written SSM Automation document within 30 minutes of the GuardDuty finding alert to collect evidence before it expires
Create a single Lambda function that sequentially calls all required APIs for evidence collection when triggered by GuardDuty SNS notification, with a timeout set to 15 minutes

Configure an EventBridge rule matching HIGH/CRITICAL GuardDuty findings to trigger a Step Functions state machine that runs parallel Lambda functions collecting EC2 metadata, security group details, VPC Flow Log query results via Athena, and IAM credential report, then stores all evidence in an S3 forensics bucket
explain: Step Functions with parallel state execution is ideal for collecting multiple evidence types simultaneously. Each Lambda handles one evidence collection task (EC2 metadata, security groups, Athena VPC Flow Log query, IAM credential report), running in parallel to minimize total collection time. Results are aggregated and stored in S3. Security Hub automated response playbooks are limited in scope and not designed for comprehensive multi-source evidence collection. A sequential Lambda risks hitting the 15-minute timeout when collecting all evidence types in series. Manual collection by an on-call analyst introduces human delay and consistency issues.

q: A company uses SQS as a message queue between microservices. They need to ensure that messages are not lost if a consuming Lambda function fails during processing. Messages must be retried up to 3 times before being moved to a dead-letter queue. Which configuration achieves this?
options: Enable SQS FIFO queues with content-based deduplication and set the message retention period to 24 hours, allowing failed messages to be replayed from the queue
Configure Lambda with a destination for asynchronous invocation failure, pointing to an SQS DLQ, and set the retry configuration to 3 maximum retries with exponential backoff

Configure the SQS queue with a visibility timeout longer than the Lambda maximum execution time, set maxReceiveCount to 3 on the redrive policy, and configure a dead-letter queue; Lambda must not delete the message until processing is confirmed successful
Enable SQS long polling with a 20-second wait time and configure the Lambda event source mapping with parallelization factor 1 to process messages sequentially with automatic retry
explain: SQS visibility timeout ensures a message being processed is hidden from other consumers. If Lambda fails, the visibility timeout expires and the message becomes visible again for retry. The redrive policy's maxReceiveCount limits retries to 3 before the message is moved to the DLQ. Lambda must not call DeleteMessage until processing succeeds — with SQS Lambda triggers, the event source mapping handles deletion on successful Lambda return. FIFO queues add ordering guarantees but do not change retry behavior. Lambda asynchronous destinations handle Lambda-invocation-level retries, not SQS visibility-based retries. Long polling improves efficiency but does not control retry count.

q: A company wants to implement policy-as-code for their CodePipeline deployments. Before any CloudFormation stack is deployed to production, it must pass Open Policy Agent (OPA) rules that enforce: no security groups with 0.0.0.0/0 ingress, all S3 buckets must have versioning enabled, and all IAM roles must use condition keys. Which CodeBuild integration pattern implements this OPA gate?
options: Package OPA as a CodeBuild environment variable; pass the CloudFormation template as a string parameter and evaluate policies using the OPA REST API

Install OPA binary in the CodeBuild pre_build phase; run opa eval with the CloudFormation template JSON and the policy files stored in CodeCommit; fail the build if any policy evaluation returns false
Use AWS Config Rules with custom Lambda functions that evaluate the same policies as OPA; trigger the Config Rules evaluation during the CodeBuild stage and read the compliance status

Use AWS CloudFormation Guard (cfn-guard) with guard rules equivalent to the OPA policies; CloudFormation Guard is natively integrated with CodeBuild and evaluates templates without requiring OPA installation
explain: The OPA binary can be downloaded and installed in the CodeBuild pre_build phase (or pre-packaged in a custom build image). The build phase runs opa eval passing the CloudFormation template as input data and the policy files (stored in CodeCommit, S3, or bundled with the source) as the policy path. OPA returns exit code 1 when policies fail, causing CodeBuild to mark the action FAILED and halt the pipeline. Policy files are version-controlled alongside application code. Passing templates as environment variable strings hits length limits. AWS Config Rules are evaluated on deployed resources, not pre-deployment templates. CloudFormation Guard is a valid alternative for this use case but cfn-guard is not 'natively integrated' with CodeBuild — it requires the same download/install pattern as OPA.

q: A team manages AWS infrastructure with Terraform and wants to enforce that the Terraform plan never adds more than 10 new resources in a single apply, preventing large-scale accidental changes. Which mechanism enforces this constraint in the CI/CD pipeline?
options: Configure a Terraform workspace policy in Terraform Cloud that limits the number of resource additions per apply; Terraform Cloud enforces this limit before allowing the apply
Use AWS Config to monitor the rate of resource creation; if more than 10 resources are created in a single hour, Config triggers an SNS alert and rolls back the Terraform apply
Add a CodeBuild step that runs terraform plan -json, parses the JSON output to count resources with action=create, and fails the build if the count exceeds 10
Use AWS Service Quotas to limit the total number of resources per account; when the limit is reached Terraform cannot add more resources, implicitly enforcing a cap
explain: terraform plan -json outputs a structured JSON document containing all planned resource changes including the action type (create, update, delete, no-op) for each resource. A CodeBuild script can parse this JSON using jq or Python to count resources where change.actions contains create and fail with a non-zero exit code if the count exceeds 10. This is a practical, pre-apply safety gate that prevents large accidental changes without custom infrastructure. Terraform Cloud workspace policies exist but are a Terraform Cloud/Enterprise feature, not applicable to self-managed Terraform pipelines. AWS Config evaluates deployed resources reactively, after resources are created. Service Quotas are per-resource-type limits set at account level, not per-apply change limits.

q: A team wants to monitor their Amazon ECS Fargate services using CloudWatch Container Insights. They need to see CPU and memory utilization at the task and container levels. What must be enabled to collect these metrics?
options: Configure AWS X-Ray in each container and enable the ContainerMetrics subsegment type to capture CPU and memory utilization data in trace segments
Enable ECS task-level CloudWatch metrics by adding a PutMetricData API call in the container entrypoint script to publish custom CPU and memory metrics

Enable Container Insights on the ECS cluster by setting the containerInsights setting to enabled in the cluster configuration, which automatically collects Fargate task and container-level metrics without agent installation
Install the CloudWatch agent as an additional sidecar container in each ECS task definition to collect container-level metrics from the Fargate host
explain: For ECS Fargate, enabling Container Insights at the cluster level (via the cluster's containerInsights setting or account-level setting) automatically activates metric collection. Unlike EC2-based ECS, Fargate does not require a DaemonSet or sidecar agent — AWS manages the underlying infrastructure and collects metrics on behalf of customers. Installing a CloudWatch agent sidecar is the approach for ECS on EC2, not Fargate. X-Ray provides tracing data, not infrastructure metrics. Custom PutMetricData scripts require significant custom development.

q: A development team accidentally deletes a production DynamoDB table that has DynamoDB Streams enabled but no point-in-time recovery (PITR) configured. What data recovery options are available?
options: Restore from the most recent on-demand backup if one was created, or check if automated backups were configured via AWS Backup; if no backups exist, the table data is permanently lost and cannot be recovered from DynamoDB Streams alone since Streams retain data for only 24 hours
Use DynamoDB Streams to replay all changes and reconstruct the table from the beginning of the stream's 24-hour retention window
Contact AWS Support to restore the deleted table from AWS internal backups which are maintained for all DynamoDB tables for 30 days
Use the DynamoDB table's CloudTrail data events to reconstruct all PutItem and DeleteItem operations and rebuild the table contents
explain: Without PITR or on-demand backups, DynamoDB table recovery options are severely limited. On-demand backups (if created) can restore the table to a specific point. AWS Backup with DynamoDB support provides automated backups. DynamoDB Streams only retain data for 24 hours and contain only the changes (not the full table scan), making complete reconstruction from streams alone impractical and likely incomplete. AWS does not maintain customer data backups accessible through support after table deletion. CloudTrail data events capture API calls but not the full item values in most cases, and cannot reconstruct a complete table.

q: A company needs to implement health checks for a multi-region Route 53 failover setup. The primary application in us-east-1 has an internal ALB not accessible from the internet. Route 53 health checkers are internet-based and cannot reach the internal ALB. How should they configure the health check?
options: Configure the Route 53 health check to check the EC2 instance public IPs directly in the target group, bypassing the internal ALB

Create a CloudWatch alarm on the ALB's HealthyHostCount metric in us-east-1, then create a Route 53 health check of type CloudWatch metric alarm that monitors this alarm state, enabling Route 53 to check internal ALB health via CloudWatch
Use AWS Global Accelerator as the health check endpoint, which can reach internal ALBs through the AWS private network and report health to Route 53
Place a public-facing proxy EC2 instance in us-east-1 that forwards health check requests to the internal ALB and returns the result, then configure Route 53 to health check the proxy
explain: Route 53 supports CloudWatch metric alarm health checks, which evaluate the state of a CloudWatch alarm rather than making direct HTTP requests. A CloudWatch alarm on ALB HealthyHostCount > 0 accurately reflects the ALB's health from within the VPC. Route 53 reads this alarm state without needing internet access to the internal ALB. A public proxy creates a maintenance burden and security exposure. Checking EC2 instance IPs directly bypasses the ALB and doesn't test the full application stack. Global Accelerator endpoints are public-facing and don't directly expose internal ALB health to Route 53.

q: A DevOps team deploys a containerized application to ECS using CodeDeploy blue/green. They need to run database schema migrations before the new application version receives traffic, but after the new task set is created and healthy. Which CodeDeploy lifecycle hook is the correct placement for database migrations?
options: Install, which is the standard hook for running setup commands including database migrations before service startup
AfterAllowTraffic, which runs after production traffic is shifted to the new task set — safe for post-deployment operations but risks serving requests before the migration completes
BeforeAllowTraffic, which runs after the replacement task set passes health checks but before traffic is shifted to it — the new tasks are running and can execute migrations against the database
BeforeInstall, which runs before ECS creates the replacement task set and is the only hook that can execute commands before any new tasks are running
explain: The BeforeAllowTraffic hook in a CodeDeploy ECS blue/green deployment fires after the replacement task set has been created, registered with the load balancer, and passed health checks — but before CodeDeploy shifts any production traffic to it. This is the optimal window for database migrations: the new application code is deployed and running (so it can connect to the database and execute migrations), but no user traffic has reached the new version yet, preventing requests from hitting an application that expects a newer schema than currently exists. BeforeInstall runs before the new task set exists — the new container code is not yet running. AfterAllowTraffic risks serving requests against unmigrated schema. Install is an EC2/on-premises hook not applicable to ECS deployments.

q: A company uses AWS Organizations with multiple OUs and accounts. They need to apply different AWS Config rules to different OUs: strict production rules for the Prod OU and relaxed rules for the Dev OU. A Config aggregator must show compliance across all accounts. Which deployment achieves per-OU Config rule configuration?
options: Enable AWS Security Hub in all accounts and use Security Hub's finding filters to simulate different compliance standards per OU; the aggregator view shows filtered findings by account type

Use CloudFormation StackSets with the SERVICE_MANAGED deployment model; create separate StackSets for production and development Config rules, each targeting the respective OU; the aggregator collects compliance data from all accounts regardless of which StackSet deployed the rules
Configure AWS Config recording in each account individually with account-specific rule sets; use a Config aggregator to centralize compliance data without requiring StackSets or organization features
Use the AWS Config organization-wide rules feature (organization Config rules) with OU-level targeting; Config applies different rule sets to different OUs using OU path conditions in the rule scope
explain: CloudFormation StackSets with SERVICE_MANAGED deployment and Organizations integration allows targeting individual OUs. Creating one StackSet with strict production Config rules targeting the Prod OU and a second StackSet with relaxed development rules targeting the Dev OU applies different configurations to different OUs automatically. New accounts added to each OU receive the appropriate rule set via auto-deployment. A central AWS Config aggregator with Organizations authorization collects compliance data from all accounts regardless of how rules were deployed. AWS Config organization rules apply uniformly to all accounts in the organization — they don't support per-OU rule differentiation natively. Security Hub aggregates security findings, not Config compliance data. Per-account manual configuration scales poorly and loses the automation benefit.

q: A DevOps engineer is creating a CloudWatch alarm for an SQS queue's ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible metric. The alarm should enter ALARM state only after the queue depth exceeds 1000 for 10 consecutive minutes, but should also alarm immediately if depth exceeds 10000. Which configuration achieves both behaviors?
options: Configure a CloudWatch alarm with dynamic threshold using anomaly detection that automatically adjusts to detect both gradual buildup and sudden spikes
Create a CloudWatch alarm with two threshold bands using metric math: if value > 10000 then 1 else if value > 1000 then counter/10, alarm when expression equals 1
Create a single CloudWatch alarm with threshold 1000 and evaluation period 10 minutes using the maximum statistic so any minute hitting 10000 triggers the alarm

Create two CloudWatch alarms — one with threshold 1000 and datapoints-to-alarm 10/10, and one with threshold 10000 and datapoints-to-alarm 1/1 — then create a composite alarm with OR logic combining both
explain: Two separate alarms with different thresholds and datapoints-to-alarm configurations handle the two distinct scenarios. The first alarm (1000 threshold, 10/10 datapoints) ensures sustained buildup triggers after 10 consecutive minutes. The second (10000 threshold, 1/1 datapoints) triggers immediately on a spike. A composite alarm with OR logic combines them into a single actionable alarm. A single alarm with maximum statistic over 10 minutes would only alarm if 10000 is sustained. CloudWatch does not support multi-band threshold expressions in a single alarm. Anomaly detection handles historical baselines but not fixed absolute thresholds.

q: A company's application uses SQS and Lambda. A bug causes Lambda to process messages incorrectly, corrupting data in DynamoDB. The bug is fixed, but 50,000 messages in the DynamoDB table need to be reprocessed correctly. How should they replay the original messages?
options: Use AWS Glue to scan the corrupted DynamoDB table, identify and filter incorrectly processed records, and rewrite the correct values based on business logic in a Glue ETL job
Use DynamoDB's built-in rollback feature to revert the table to its state before the Lambda bug was introduced

If DynamoDB Streams was enabled, use Kinesis Data Firehose or a Lambda consumer of the stream with a trim horizon iterator to replay historical change events; otherwise restore from a DynamoDB backup taken before the corruption and use DynamoDB Streams to replay only the changes since the backup
Manually re-enter the 50,000 records into DynamoDB by exporting the original SQS messages from the SQS queue's DLQ and reprocessing them through the fixed Lambda
explain: DynamoDB Streams retain up to 24 hours of changes. Using a trim horizon (TRIM_HORIZON) iterator replays changes from the beginning of the retention window. If the corruption happened within 24 hours, stream replay through a corrected Lambda can reprocess and fix records. For older corruption, restoring from a backup provides a clean starting point, then replaying subsequent streams brings the table to the correct post-corruption state. Manual re-entry of 50,000 records is impractical. DynamoDB does not have a built-in rollback feature. Glue ETL can work for data transformation but requires custom logic to identify and fix corrupted records without the original source values.

q: A microservices application on EKS needs to maintain 99.9% availability for each service. Kubernetes deployments currently have 1 replica. What Kubernetes and AWS configurations ensure high availability at the pod and node level?
options: Enable Kubernetes horizontal pod autoscaling targeting 70% CPU to scale from 1 to 10 replicas based on load, ensuring pods are always available during traffic spikes
Configure EKS managed node groups with spot instances across 3 instance types and 3 AZs, using the on-demand base capacity for critical services

Set each deployment to minimum 3 replicas with a Pod Disruption Budget of maxUnavailable: 1, configure EKS node groups across 3 AZs with cluster autoscaler, and use pod topology spread constraints to distribute pods across AZs
Deploy EKS Fargate profiles for all services to eliminate node management and rely on AWS-managed infrastructure availability guarantees
explain: High availability requires redundancy at multiple levels: multiple pod replicas (minimum 3 ensures one can fail while maintaining N-1 availability), Pod Disruption Budgets prevent voluntary disruptions from taking down too many pods simultaneously, node groups across 3 AZs protect against AZ failure, and topology spread constraints distribute pods across AZs preventing co-location failures. HPA scales based on load but starts at 1 replica and takes time to scale — 1 replica always has a single point of failure. Spot instances improve cost but have interruption risk without careful on-demand base capacity. Fargate eliminates node management but doesn't automatically provide multi-AZ pod distribution.

q: A team has a monorepo containing 8 microservices. They use CodePipeline and want to rebuild and redeploy only the services whose source files changed in a given commit, rather than rebuilding all 8 on every push. Which architecture implements selective microservice rebuilding?
options: Configure one CodePipeline per microservice with a CodeCommit source trigger; use EventBridge to filter push events by changed file paths and only trigger the pipelines corresponding to changed service directories

Configure a single CodePipeline with a CodeBuild action that reads the git diff, identifies changed service directories, and uses the AWS CLI to trigger the specific per-service pipelines via codepipeline:StartPipelineExecution for only the affected services
Use AWS CodeBuild batch builds with a buildspec.yml at the repo root that defines a build matrix; CodeBuild automatically determines which matrix entries to run based on changed files

Use a Lambda function triggered on CodeCommit push events that reads the changed files from the commit using codecommit:GetDifferences, determines which services are affected, and calls codepipeline:StartPipelineExecution for each affected service pipeline
explain: A Lambda function triggered by a CodeCommit (or GitHub via EventBridge) push event has access to the commit details. It calls codecommit:GetDifferences to retrieve the list of changed files, applies path-prefix matching to determine which service directories were modified, and calls codepipeline:StartPipelineExecution only for the corresponding per-service pipelines. Each service maintains its own independent pipeline for isolated deployments. This approach is event-driven, requires no polling, and triggers only the necessary pipelines. Per-service pipelines with EventBridge file-path filtering are not natively supported by EventBridge for CodeCommit. A single monolithic pipeline with selective triggering logic in CodeBuild still executes the full pipeline for every push. CodeBuild batch builds run matrix entries regardless of which files changed — file-change-based matrix filtering is not a native feature.

q: A DevOps team stores sensitive database credentials in AWS Secrets Manager with automatic rotation every 30 days. The rotation Lambda function is configured but the team notices rotation is silently failing — credentials in Secrets Manager are not being updated and the old password is still active. What is the most common cause of Secrets Manager rotation failures?
options: The 30-day rotation period is too short for the database password policy, which requires passwords to be at least 90 days old before changing

The rotation Lambda function cannot reach the database or Secrets Manager API due to network configuration issues — for Lambda functions in a VPC, the function needs a route to the Secrets Manager API endpoint (via VPC endpoint or NAT gateway) and to the database
The rotation Lambda function's IAM execution role is missing the secretsmanager:RotateSecret permission required to update the secret value during rotation
Secrets Manager rotation requires the RDS instance to have Multi-AZ enabled; rotation fails silently on Single-AZ RDS instances because the rotation Lambda cannot connect to the standby replica
explain: The most common cause of silent Secrets Manager rotation failures is network connectivity. Rotation Lambda functions that run inside a VPC must have network access to both the target database (to change the password) and the Secrets Manager service endpoint (to update the secret value). Without a Secrets Manager VPC endpoint or a NAT gateway/internet gateway route, the Lambda function times out attempting to call the Secrets Manager API, and the rotation fails silently. The Lambda execution logs in CloudWatch reveal timeout errors. A Secrets Manager VPC endpoint (com.amazonaws.region.secretsmanager) eliminates the need for a NAT gateway for the Secrets Manager API call. The IAM permission required is secretsmanager:RotateSecret but also needs secretsmanager:GetSecretValue and secretsmanager:PutSecretValue for the rotation workflow. Password minimum age policies are a less common cause. Multi-AZ is not required for rotation.

q: A security team wants to detect when IAM root account credentials are used in any AWS account across their Organization. They need near-real-time notification within 2 minutes of the event. Which architecture meets the requirement?
options: Enable AWS Security Hub with the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark standard, which includes a check for root account usage and sends findings to the security team via email
Configure GuardDuty across all accounts with delegated administration and enable the RootCredentialUsage finding type to alert via SNS

Configure an organization-level CloudTrail trail delivering to CloudWatch Logs, create a metric filter matching { $.userIdentity.type = 'Root' }, create a CloudWatch Alarm on the metric, and configure SNS to notify the security team
Create an EventBridge rule in the management account that matches ConsoleLogin events from root user type and sends to an SNS topic
explain: The CloudTrail → CloudWatch Logs → metric filter → alarm → SNS pipeline achieves near-real-time detection (typically under 2 minutes) for root credential usage events captured in CloudTrail. The metric filter pattern { $.userIdentity.type = 'Root' } matches all root account API calls and console logins. Security Hub CIS Benchmark checks run periodically, not in real-time. GuardDuty does not have a RootCredentialUsage finding type as a standard detection. EventBridge rules on CloudTrail events only work if CloudTrail is configured as an event source, and only for management events in the same account by default.

q: A company needs to ensure their incident response runbooks are tested regularly and findings are tracked over time. They want to measure mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to resolve (MTTR) for each incident type. Which AWS service provides incident metrics tracking?
options: Use AWS Systems Manager Incident Manager which tracks incident creation time, detection time, and resolution time, publishing metrics to CloudWatch that enable MTTD and MTTR calculation per incident type through CloudWatch Dashboards
Configure AWS Config to record all resource configuration changes and use Config configuration history to calculate how long resources were in a degraded state
Use AWS CloudWatch Logs to capture incident responder activity and run Logs Insights queries to calculate time intervals between key log entries for MTTD and MTTR
Create a custom DynamoDB table where incident responders manually log incident start, detection, and resolution timestamps for metric calculation in a separate analytics pipeline
explain: AWS Systems Manager Incident Manager natively tracks incident lifecycle timestamps including creation, detection, and resolution times. It publishes these as CloudWatch metrics, enabling CloudWatch Dashboard widgets that display MTTD and MTTR trends by incident type over time. CloudWatch Alarms can alert when these metrics exceed acceptable thresholds. A manual DynamoDB tracking table requires discipline from responders under pressure and has no automatic data collection. CloudWatch Logs activity parsing requires responders to log precisely formatted entries during incidents. Config configuration history measures resource state duration, not incident response performance.

q: A company has an Aurora Global Database with a primary cluster in us-east-1 and a secondary cluster in eu-west-1. During a disaster where us-east-1 becomes completely unavailable, the team needs to promote the eu-west-1 cluster to primary. What is the correct procedure and expected RPO?
options: Promote an Aurora read replica in eu-west-1 to a standalone cluster using the PromoteReadReplica API; RPO depends on asynchronous replication lag which can be minutes
Scale up the Aurora secondary cluster in eu-west-1 to the same instance size as the primary, then update the application's connection string to the secondary endpoint
Take a manual snapshot of the primary cluster in us-east-1, copy it to eu-west-1, and restore from the snapshot; RPO equals the time since the last automated snapshot

Perform a managed planned failover or unplanned failover (detach from global database and promote) in the eu-west-1 cluster; Aurora Global Database typically replicates with sub-second latency, so RPO is usually less than 1 second under normal conditions
explain: Aurora Global Database replicates using dedicated infrastructure with typically sub-1-second replication lag, measured in the GlobalWriteForwardingLatency metric. In a disaster scenario, you promote the secondary by removing it from the global database (detach) and converting it to a standalone writer cluster. For a planned failover, Aurora provides a managed failover with zero data loss. The RPO is typically under 1 second under normal replication conditions. Snapshot-based restore has RPO measured in hours. Standard read replicas use asynchronous replication with potentially minutes of lag. Scaling instances alone does not make the secondary writable.

q: A DevOps team uses AWS CodePipeline with GitHub as the source. They notice that the pipeline triggers on every commit to main, including documentation-only changes (README, docs/ folder). They want to skip pipeline execution for commits that only modify files outside the src/ directory. Which solution filters pipeline triggers by changed file paths?
options: Configure an EventBridge rule between GitHub and CodePipeline that matches push events and uses an EventBridge event pattern content filter to check the changed file list before routing to CodePipeline

Use a CodeBuild action in the first pipeline stage that checks git diff --name-only against the previous commit and exits 0 with a skip signal if only non-src files changed, preventing downstream stages from executing

Add a Lambda function as the first pipeline action that checks the source commit's changed files using the GitHub API; the Lambda calls codepipeline:PutJobSuccessResult to continue or codepipeline:StopPipelineExecution to halt for doc-only commits

Add a condition in the CodePipeline source action configuration using the FilePaths filter property available in GitHub source actions via CodeStar Connections, which triggers the pipeline only when files matching src/** are changed
explain: AWS CodePipeline's GitHub (via CodeStar Connections) source action supports a FilePaths trigger filter that specifies include/exclude glob patterns for file paths. When configured with an include pattern of src/**, the pipeline only triggers when at least one changed file matches the pattern. Commits modifying only documentation files in docs/ or README.md do not match the filter and do not trigger a pipeline execution. This is a native, zero-code solution. EventBridge event pattern filtering does not inspect the list of changed files in GitHub push events at that granularity. Lambda-based filtering adds a stage that always executes and requires GitHub API access. A CodeBuild file-check stage executes on every commit and uses StopPipelineExecution, which still consumes pipeline execution resources.

q: A company's AWS environment has grown organically with hundreds of ad-hoc CloudFormation stacks deployed over several years. Many stacks have drifted significantly from their templates. The team wants to bring all infrastructure under disciplined IaC management, but re-importing and writing templates for hundreds of stacks is daunting. Which approach migrates existing resources to IaC management most efficiently?
options: Use the AWS CloudFormation IaC Generator (available in CloudFormation console) to scan existing resources in the account, select the resources to include, and generate a CloudFormation template and resource import configuration; then import the resources into a new managed stack

Write a custom script using the AWS SDK to call Describe* APIs for each resource type, extract properties, and generate CloudFormation templates; this provides the most accurate template generation compared to AWS-native tools

Use AWS CloudFormation's cloudformation:ImportResource feature to import all unmanaged resources into new stacks by specifying their resource type and physical ID; CloudFormation generates the required template automatically
Use AWS Migration Hub to inventory existing resources and generate CloudFormation templates; Migration Hub's IaC generator produces importable templates from the existing resource configurations
explain: AWS CloudFormation IaC Generator is a feature that scans existing AWS resources in an account, allows selecting which resources to include, and generates both a CloudFormation template and an import configuration file. This accelerates the migration of existing unmanaged resources to CloudFormation management by automating the template writing step. After generation, the team uses cloudformation:ImportResource to import the resources into a new stack under the generated template's logical IDs. ImportResource alone requires a pre-written template — it doesn't generate one. AWS Migration Hub focuses on application migration tracking, not IaC template generation. A custom SDK script requires significant development effort and must handle every resource type's property mapping individually.

q: A team needs to debug Lambda function performance issues where functions occasionally take 30+ seconds. AWS X-Ray traces show the total duration but do not reveal which internal operations are slow. What change enables identifying the slow code path?
options: Enable X-Ray active tracing with 100% sampling rate and increase the X-Ray trace retention period to capture all slow invocations for later analysis
Configure Lambda Power Tuning to test the function at different memory sizes, which will identify memory-constrained operations causing slowness

Add custom X-Ray subsegments around each major code block using AWSXRay.captureFunc() or the equivalent SDK method to annotate timing for database calls, external API calls, and business logic sections
